description: Navigate questions of faith — dealing with doubt, finding reliable sources, and strengthening testimony.

1.definition: It's okay to have questions about God and the gospel — that's how you learn!
1.scenarioDoneTo: During Primary, you wonder why Heavenly Father doesn't just talk to everyone out loud. You're not sure if it's okay to ask.
1.scenarioYouDo: During Primary, you wonder why Heavenly Father doesn't just talk to everyone out loud. You're not sure if it's okay to ask.

challenge.scenario: During Primary, you wonder why Heavenly Father doesn't just talk to everyone out loud. You're not sure if it's okay to ask.
options: Keep your question to yourself because it might be wrong to ask
Raise your hand and ask your teacher — questions help you learn
Decide you'll never understand and stop trying

1.tip: Asking questions is one of the bravest things you can do — Heavenly Father loves when you want to learn! (James 1:5)

2.definition: Having genuine questions about your faith is a healthy, normal part of growing spiritually.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A friend at church says they feel guilty because they have doubts about whether God really answers prayers. They ask you if something is wrong with them.
2.scenarioYouDo: A friend at church says they feel guilty because they have doubts about whether God really answers prayers. They ask you if something is wrong with them.

challenge.scenario: A friend at church says they feel guilty because they have doubts about whether God really answers prayers. They ask you if something is wrong with them.
options: Tell them doubts mean they're losing their testimony
Remind them that having questions is normal and can actually help their faith grow
Suggest they just stop thinking so much about it
Say that truly faithful people never have doubts

2.tip: Questions aren't the enemy of faith — they're often the beginning of deeper understanding. (Alma 32:27)

3.definition: Sincere questions about doctrine, history, or practice are an expected part of spiritual maturity — not a sign of spiritual failure.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A returned missionary in your institute class shares that they've been wrestling with difficult questions about church history since coming home. Several classmates question their faithfulness.
3.scenarioYouDo: A returned missionary in your institute class shares that they've been wrestling with difficult questions about church history since coming home. Several classmates question their faithfulness.

challenge.scenario: A returned missionary in your institute class shares that they've been wrestling with difficult questions about church history since coming home. Several classmates question their faithfulness.
options: Agree that missionaries shouldn't come home with questions
Tell the missionary to just stop reading about history
Affirm that wrestling with questions is part of a maturing faith and encourage them to keep seeking answers
Suggest they were probably not a very faithful missionary

3.tip: A faith that has room for hard questions is a faith strong enough to grow. (Doctrine and Covenants 88:118)

1.definition: Joseph Smith was just a young boy when he asked God a question — and God answered him.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You're learning about the First Vision and find out Joseph Smith was only 14 when he prayed in the Sacred Grove. A friend says, 'God wouldn't answer a kid.'
1.scenarioYouDo: You're learning about the First Vision and find out Joseph Smith was only 14 when he prayed in the Sacred Grove. A friend says, 'God wouldn't answer a kid.'

challenge.scenario: You're learning about the First Vision and find out Joseph Smith was only 14 when he prayed in the Sacred Grove. A friend says, 'God wouldn't answer a kid.'
options: Your friend is right — God only talks to grown-ups
God probably didn't really answer Joseph
Joseph Smith showed that God answers sincere prayers, no matter how old you are

1.tip: One boy's sincere question changed the world — your questions matter too! (Joseph Smith—History 1:15-17)

2.definition: The Restoration of the gospel began because a young man took his question to God in sincere prayer.
2.scenarioDoneTo: In seminary, your teacher challenges everyone to follow Joseph Smith's example and take their biggest spiritual question to God in prayer. You're not sure God would answer you.
2.scenarioYouDo: In seminary, your teacher challenges everyone to follow Joseph Smith's example and take their biggest spiritual question to God in prayer. You're not sure God would answer you.

challenge.scenario: In seminary, your teacher challenges everyone to follow Joseph Smith's example and take their biggest spiritual question to God in prayer. You're not sure God would answer you.
options: God promised to answer anyone who asks sincerely — try it
Only special people like prophets get answers from God
Prayer doesn't really work for regular questions
Wait until your question is important enough to bother God with

2.tip: If God would answer a fourteen-year-old farm boy, He'll answer you too. (James 1:5-6)

3.definition: Joseph Smith's First Vision demonstrates that sincere, faith-filled inquiry — not passive acceptance — is the pattern God established for receiving revelation.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A classmate argues that Joseph Smith's experience was unique and that ordinary members shouldn't expect personal revelation the same way. They say the church has all the answers already.
3.scenarioYouDo: A classmate argues that Joseph Smith's experience was unique and that ordinary members shouldn't expect personal revelation the same way. They say the church has all the answers already.

challenge.scenario: A classmate argues that Joseph Smith's experience was unique and that ordinary members shouldn't expect personal revelation the same way. They say the church has all the answers already.
options: Agree — personal revelation was just for the prophet, not ordinary members
Explain that the pattern of asking God and receiving personal revelation applies to all sincere seekers, not just prophets
Suggest they're right because ordinary members wouldn't know how to interpret revelation
Say that since the church already has every answer, there's no reason to pray about things

3.tip: The First Vision wasn't just about founding a church — it established a pattern for every person to approach God directly. (Moroni 10:4-5)

1.definition: There's a difference between asking because you really want to know and asking just to cause trouble.
1.scenarioDoneTo: During family scripture study, your older sibling keeps asking questions about why the scriptures are 'weird' and rolls their eyes at every answer. You have a real question too.
1.scenarioYouDo: During family scripture study, your older sibling keeps asking questions about why the scriptures are 'weird' and rolls their eyes at every answer. You have a real question too.

challenge.scenario: During family scripture study, your older sibling keeps asking questions about why the scriptures are 'weird' and rolls their eyes at every answer. You have a real question too.
options: Ask your question with a sincere heart — you really want to understand
Copy your sibling and make fun of the scriptures too
Don't ask anything because all questions seem bad now

1.tip: When your heart really wants to know the truth, that's the kind of question Heavenly Father loves to answer. (Moroni 10:4)

2.definition: A sincere question seeks understanding, while a cynical question seeks to tear down — and the Spirit responds to the difference.
2.scenarioDoneTo: In Sunday School, one person asks, 'How can I understand the Atonement better?' and another says, 'How can anyone actually believe this stuff?' Both are questions, but they feel very different.
2.scenarioYouDo: In Sunday School, one person asks, 'How can I understand the Atonement better?' and another says, 'How can anyone actually believe this stuff?' Both are questions, but they feel very different.

challenge.scenario: In Sunday School, one person asks, 'How can I understand the Atonement better?' and another says, 'How can anyone actually believe this stuff?' Both are questions, but they feel very different.
options: There's no real difference — a question is a question
Both questions deserve the same response because all questions are equal
The first question comes from sincere intent and is more likely to invite the Spirit, while the second is designed to dismiss
Asking difficult questions always means you're being cynical

2.tip: Your intent shapes the answer you'll receive. Seek to understand, not to undermine. (3 Nephi 14:7-8)

3.definition: Discerning between sincere inquiry and cynical challenge is important — one invites revelation while the other closes the door to it.
3.scenarioDoneTo: On a faith-related online forum, you see someone asking 'gotcha' questions about the Book of Mormon — not genuinely looking for answers, but trying to embarrass believers. You also see someone asking similar questions with genuine curiosity and humility.
3.scenarioYouDo: On a faith-related online forum, you see someone asking 'gotcha' questions about the Book of Mormon — not genuinely looking for answers, but trying to embarrass believers. You also see someone asking similar questions with genuine curiosity and humility.

challenge.scenario: On a faith-related online forum, you see someone asking 'gotcha' questions about the Book of Mormon — not genuinely looking for answers, but trying to embarrass believers. You also see someone asking similar questions with genuine curiosity and humility.
options: Both people deserve the same response because the questions are identical
The tone and intent behind a question don't matter — only the facts do
You should avoid all online discussions about faith because questions are dangerous
Recognize the difference in intent and engage thoughtfully with sincere seekers without letting cynical questions disturb your peace

3.tip: The same question asked with two different hearts gets two very different outcomes. (Matthew 7:6)

1.definition: When you ask Heavenly Father a question, be ready to listen and follow what He says.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You pray and ask if the Book of Mormon is true, but you already decided you don't want to read it no matter what the answer is.
1.scenarioYouDo: You pray and ask if the Book of Mormon is true, but you already decided you don't want to read it no matter what the answer is.

challenge.scenario: You pray and ask if the Book of Mormon is true, but you already decided you don't want to read it no matter what the answer is.
options: That's fine — just asking is enough
It's better to ask with a real desire to follow what Heavenly Father tells you
You don't need to do anything after praying — God does everything for you

1.tip: Real intent means you're ready to act on the answer — not just hear it. (Moroni 10:4)

2.definition: God expects us to ask with 'real intent' — a willingness to act on the answer we receive.
2.scenarioDoneTo: Your seminary teacher explains Moroni's promise and emphasizes 'real intent.' Your friend says, 'I prayed once and didn't feel anything, so it doesn't work.'
2.scenarioYouDo: Your seminary teacher explains Moroni's promise and emphasizes 'real intent.' Your friend says, 'I prayed once and didn't feel anything, so it doesn't work.'

challenge.scenario: Your seminary teacher explains Moroni's promise and emphasizes 'real intent.' Your friend says, 'I prayed once and didn't feel anything, so it doesn't work.'
options: Agree — if God didn't answer the first time, He probably won't ever
Suggest they keep asking but never expect to actually change anything
Tell them Moroni's promise doesn't work for most people
Ask them if they were truly willing to act on an answer, and encourage them to try again with real intent

2.tip: Real intent isn't just wanting an answer — it's being willing to follow wherever that answer leads. (Moroni 10:4)

3.definition: Asking with real intent means approaching God with genuine willingness to accept and act on His answer — even if it's not what you expected.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You've been praying about a major life decision, but you realize you've been hoping God will just confirm what you already want to do rather than being open to a different answer.
3.scenarioYouDo: You've been praying about a major life decision, but you realize you've been hoping God will just confirm what you already want to do rather than being open to a different answer.

challenge.scenario: You've been praying about a major life decision, but you realize you've been hoping God will just confirm what you already want to do rather than being open to a different answer.
options: Acknowledge your bias and recommit to being genuinely open to God's will, even if it surprises you
Keep praying the same way — eventually God will agree with you
Stop praying about it and just do what you want
Decide that if God hasn't confirmed your plan, He must not care about your decision

3.tip: Real intent means holding your plans with open hands and letting God redirect if needed. (Doctrine and Covenants 9:7-8)

1.definition: You can believe in Heavenly Father and still have questions — both can be true at the same time.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You believe in Jesus but sometimes wonder why bad things happen. A friend says, 'If you really believed, you wouldn't wonder about that.'
1.scenarioYouDo: You believe in Jesus but sometimes wonder why bad things happen. A friend says, 'If you really believed, you wouldn't wonder about that.'

challenge.scenario: You believe in Jesus but sometimes wonder why bad things happen. A friend says, 'If you really believed, you wouldn't wonder about that.'
options: Your friend is probably right — stop wondering
You should pick one: either believe everything or question everything
It's totally okay to believe AND have questions at the same time

1.tip: Even the man in the Bible said, 'I believe — help thou mine unbelief.' Faith and questions can live together. (Mark 9:24)

2.definition: Faith doesn't require having all the answers — it means trusting God while you search for them.
2.scenarioDoneTo: Your older cousin left the church and tells you that if you can't answer every hard question about church history, your faith is built on sand.
2.scenarioYouDo: Your older cousin left the church and tells you that if you can't answer every hard question about church history, your faith is built on sand.

challenge.scenario: Your older cousin left the church and tells you that if you can't answer every hard question about church history, your faith is built on sand.
options: They might be right — you need to answer every question before you can believe
Faith means trusting God while you continue to seek answers, even when you don't have them all yet
Ignore all hard questions and hope they go away
Leave the church until you can answer everything

2.tip: You don't need all the answers to have a strong testimony — just the willingness to keep seeking. (Ether 12:6)

3.definition: Mature faith embraces the tension of holding both conviction and unanswered questions — neither demanding certainty about everything nor abandoning what you already know.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You have a strong testimony of the Book of Mormon and the Atonement, but you're struggling with a specific historical issue. A podcast host says you're being intellectually dishonest if you stay in the church without resolving every concern.
3.scenarioYouDo: You have a strong testimony of the Book of Mormon and the Atonement, but you're struggling with a specific historical issue. A podcast host says you're being intellectually dishonest if you stay in the church without resolving every concern.

challenge.scenario: You have a strong testimony of the Book of Mormon and the Atonement, but you're struggling with a specific historical issue. A podcast host says you're being intellectually dishonest if you stay in the church without resolving every concern.
options: Agree — staying in the church with unresolved questions is intellectually dishonest
Leave the church until you've resolved every single question
Recognize that holding both faith and unanswered questions is intellectually honest and spiritually mature — you don't abandon what you know because of what you don't know
Decide that hard questions aren't worth exploring and simply push them out of your mind

3.tip: Intellectual honesty isn't having all the answers — it's staying open to truth from every direction, including spiritual sources. (Alma 32:21)

1.definition: When you have a question about the gospel, one of the best things you can do is pray about it.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You're not sure if Heavenly Father really hears your prayers. You don't know who to ask about it.
1.scenarioYouDo: You're not sure if Heavenly Father really hears your prayers. You don't know who to ask about it.

challenge.scenario: You're not sure if Heavenly Father really hears your prayers. You don't know who to ask about it.
options: Kneel down and pray — ask Heavenly Father to help you know He's listening
Stop praying because it probably doesn't work
Only talk to friends about it and never ask God

1.tip: Heavenly Father is always ready to hear you — especially when you have questions. (3 Nephi 18:20)

2.definition: Personal prayer is the primary channel for receiving answers to spiritual questions — take your concerns directly to God.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You've been reading online about church history and feeling confused. One friend says you should only look at what critics say, another says to only read church materials. You're caught in the middle.
2.scenarioYouDo: You've been reading online about church history and feeling confused. One friend says you should only look at what critics say, another says to only read church materials. You're caught in the middle.

challenge.scenario: You've been reading online about church history and feeling confused. One friend says you should only look at what critics say, another says to only read church materials. You're caught in the middle.
options: Only listen to critics because they're more honest about things
Only read church materials and ignore everything else
Take your confusion directly to God in prayer and ask for clarity while you continue studying from good sources
Stop researching entirely and pretend the issue doesn't exist

2.tip: People can point you in many directions, but only God can speak directly to your heart. (Doctrine and Covenants 8:2-3)

3.definition: While research, counsel, and study are valuable, taking your deepest questions to God in prayer invites revelation that no other source can provide.
3.scenarioDoneTo: After months of studying a difficult doctrinal issue, you feel like you've exhausted human resources — books, podcasts, even a conversation with a religion professor. You still don't have peace.
3.scenarioYouDo: After months of studying a difficult doctrinal issue, you feel like you've exhausted human resources — books, podcasts, even a conversation with a religion professor. You still don't have peace.

challenge.scenario: After months of studying a difficult doctrinal issue, you feel like you've exhausted human resources — books, podcasts, even a conversation with a religion professor. You still don't have peace.
options: Accept that some questions simply have no answers and stop caring
Dedicate focused, sincere prayer to present your question to God, trusting that He can provide peace and understanding beyond human resources
Conclude that if scholars can't resolve it, nobody can
Decide the issue must not matter if it's this hard to figure out

3.tip: When human wisdom runs dry, divine revelation is still flowing. Take your hardest questions to God first. (Alma 5:46)

1.definition: Sometimes answers from Heavenly Father don't come right away — and that's okay.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You prayed last night about a question, but you didn't feel an answer. You feel frustrated.
1.scenarioYouDo: You prayed last night about a question, but you didn't feel an answer. You feel frustrated.

challenge.scenario: You prayed last night about a question, but you didn't feel an answer. You feel frustrated.
options: Give up — if God didn't answer right away, He won't answer at all
Keep praying and be patient — answers sometimes take time
Decide prayer doesn't work for you

1.tip: Heavenly Father always hears you, even when the answer takes a little while. (Psalm 27:14)

2.definition: Not all spiritual answers come on our timeline — patience and continued faithfulness are part of the process.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You've been praying for weeks about whether to keep participating in church, and you haven't felt a clear answer. A friend says, 'If God wanted you to stay, He'd have told you by now.'
2.scenarioYouDo: You've been praying for weeks about whether to keep participating in church, and you haven't felt a clear answer. A friend says, 'If God wanted you to stay, He'd have told you by now.'

challenge.scenario: You've been praying for weeks about whether to keep participating in church, and you haven't felt a clear answer. A friend says, 'If God wanted you to stay, He'd have told you by now.'
options: Keep being faithful and patient — God's timing is different from yours, and the answer will come
Your friend is right — no quick answer means no answer at all
Take the silence as a sign you should just leave
Stop praying and make the decision completely on your own

2.tip: Sometimes the waiting IS the answer — God is building your faith in the pause. (Doctrine and Covenants 24:8)

3.definition: Divine answers often come incrementally and sometimes after prolonged seasons of faithful patience — demanding immediate resolution can shortchange the refining process God intends.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You've been carrying a major faith question for over a year. You've prayed, studied, attended the temple, and counseled with leaders, but you still don't have a complete answer. You're tempted to give up.
3.scenarioYouDo: You've been carrying a major faith question for over a year. You've prayed, studied, attended the temple, and counseled with leaders, but you still don't have a complete answer. You're tempted to give up.

challenge.scenario: You've been carrying a major faith question for over a year. You've prayed, studied, attended the temple, and counseled with leaders, but you still don't have a complete answer. You're tempted to give up.
options: Conclude that God isn't real because He hasn't answered on your timeline
Accept that you'll never get an answer and learn to live with permanent doubt
Set a deadline for God — if He doesn't answer by then, you're done with faith
Recognize that some answers come slowly by design, and continued faithfulness during uncertainty is itself a powerful act of trust in God

3.tip: Some of God's greatest blessings are forged in seasons of waiting. Don't let timing steal your testimony. (Isaiah 40:31)

1.definition: The scriptures and the words of prophets can help you find answers to your questions.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You have a question about why we need to be baptized. Your mom suggests looking in the scriptures together for the answer.
1.scenarioYouDo: You have a question about why we need to be baptized. Your mom suggests looking in the scriptures together for the answer.

challenge.scenario: You have a question about why we need to be baptized. Your mom suggests looking in the scriptures together for the answer.
options: Tell her the scriptures are boring and won't help
Say you'd rather just ask a friend at school instead
Read the scriptures with her — they were written to help you find answers

1.tip: The scriptures are like a treasure map — the answers are in there if you look! (2 Timothy 3:16-17)

2.definition: Consistent scripture study, listening to prophets, and seeking the Spirit work together to help answer gospel questions.
2.scenarioDoneTo: Your friend is confused about a church teaching. Instead of just reading random opinions online, your seminary teacher suggests a study approach: scriptures, conference talks, and prayer.
2.scenarioYouDo: Your friend is confused about a church teaching. Instead of just reading random opinions online, your seminary teacher suggests a study approach: scriptures, conference talks, and prayer.

challenge.scenario: Your friend is confused about a church teaching. Instead of just reading random opinions online, your seminary teacher suggests a study approach: scriptures, conference talks, and prayer.
options: That's too much work — just pick whichever online opinion sounds best
Only read one talk and call it good enough
Stop worrying about it — the answer doesn't really matter
Follow the study approach — combining scriptures, prophetic counsel, and prayer gives the fullest picture

2.tip: Answers to big questions rarely come from a single source — study widely and let the Spirit connect the dots. (2 Nephi 32:3)

3.definition: A disciplined approach to revelation — combining scripture study, prophetic counsel, and Spirit-directed pondering — is more likely to yield lasting answers than casual searching.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A friend says they've 'studied everything' about a controversial church topic but only read blog posts and watched YouTube videos. They didn't study relevant scriptures, conference talks, or the church's Gospel Topics essays.
3.scenarioYouDo: A friend says they've 'studied everything' about a controversial church topic but only read blog posts and watched YouTube videos. They didn't study relevant scriptures, conference talks, or the church's Gospel Topics essays.

challenge.scenario: A friend says they've 'studied everything' about a controversial church topic but only read blog posts and watched YouTube videos. They didn't study relevant scriptures, conference talks, or the church's Gospel Topics essays.
options: Suggest that genuine study includes primary sources like scriptures, prophetic teaching, and official church resources — alongside other perspectives
Their research is thorough enough — blogs and videos cover all the angles
Tell them only the church's own materials matter and everything else is lies
Agree that conference talks and scriptures are outdated and irrelevant to tough modern questions

3.tip: Real study isn't just consuming content — it's deliberately including the sources most likely to carry spiritual light. (Doctrine and Covenants 88:118)

1.definition: When something confuses you, hold on to the things you already know are true.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You hear something confusing about the church at school. But you remember how you felt when you prayed last week and felt the Holy Ghost.
1.scenarioYouDo: You hear something confusing about the church at school. But you remember how you felt when you prayed last week and felt the Holy Ghost.

challenge.scenario: You hear something confusing about the church at school. But you remember how you felt when you prayed last week and felt the Holy Ghost.
options: Remember what you've already felt and hold on to it while you figure out the confusing part
Forget everything you've felt before and decide the church must be wrong
Decide you can never trust your feelings again

1.tip: When things get confusing, remember what you already know — those feelings were real. (2 Nephi 31:20)

2.definition: Unanswered questions shouldn't cause you to abandon the spiritual experiences and knowledge you've already received.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You discover a difficult issue in church history and it really bothers you. But you also remember powerful spiritual experiences during seminary and while reading the Book of Mormon.
2.scenarioYouDo: You discover a difficult issue in church history and it really bothers you. But you also remember powerful spiritual experiences during seminary and while reading the Book of Mormon.

challenge.scenario: You discover a difficult issue in church history and it really bothers you. But you also remember powerful spiritual experiences during seminary and while reading the Book of Mormon.
options: Let the one issue erase everything you've ever experienced
Throw away your testimony until every question is fully answered
Hold on to your spiritual experiences — they're real — while you work through the difficult issue
Pretend the issue doesn't exist and never think about it

2.tip: Don't let what you don't know destroy what you do know. (Helaman 5:12)

3.definition: Faithful navigation of doubt means you don't let unanswered questions destroy the foundation of spiritual knowledge you've already built through sacred experiences.
3.scenarioDoneTo: After reading several critical books about the church, you feel shaken. But when you're honest with yourself, you recall specific, undeniable spiritual witnesses — feelings during temple ordinances, answers to heartfelt prayers, and moments of profound peace.
3.scenarioYouDo: After reading several critical books about the church, you feel shaken. But when you're honest with yourself, you recall specific, undeniable spiritual witnesses — feelings during temple ordinances, answers to heartfelt prayers, and moments of profound peace.

challenge.scenario: After reading several critical books about the church, you feel shaken. But when you're honest with yourself, you recall specific, undeniable spiritual witnesses — feelings during temple ordinances, answers to heartfelt prayers, and moments of profound peace.
options: Dismiss your spiritual experiences as emotional manipulation since intellectual arguments seem stronger
Abandon all faith because critical arguments are confident and well-sourced
Recognize that spiritual witnesses are a legitimate form of knowledge and hold on to them while honestly exploring your questions
Refuse to read anything challenging ever again to protect your faith

3.tip: Spiritual knowledge is real knowledge. Don't let one category of evidence erase another. (Moroni 10:5)

1.definition: Asking questions and finding answers can make your testimony even stronger.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You asked your dad why we pay tithing, and after he explained, you felt even better about it than before.
1.scenarioYouDo: You asked your dad why we pay tithing, and after he explained, you felt even better about it than before.

challenge.scenario: You asked your dad why we pay tithing, and after he explained, you felt even better about it than before.
options: Questions never help anything grow
Asking questions and getting answers made your testimony stronger!
You should have just paid tithing without ever thinking about it

1.tip: Every question you explore is a chance for your testimony to grow. (Doctrine and Covenants 59:21)

2.definition: When you wrestle with hard questions honestly and faithfully, your testimony often ends up deeper and more resilient.
2.scenarioDoneTo: After months of studying and praying about a gospel topic that confused you, you now understand it better than ever — your testimony actually feels stronger than before.
2.scenarioYouDo: After months of studying and praying about a gospel topic that confused you, you now understand it better than ever — your testimony actually feels stronger than before.

challenge.scenario: After months of studying and praying about a gospel topic that confused you, you now understand it better than ever — your testimony actually feels stronger than before.
options: That's strange — studying shouldn't make your testimony stronger
It makes sense: honestly working through questions deepens understanding and builds lasting faith
You probably just convinced yourself — real testimony doesn't need studying
You should stop studying before you find something that actually shakes your faith

2.tip: A testimony that has survived honest questions is stronger than one that was never tested. (Ether 12:6)

3.definition: Questions pursued with faith and intellectual honesty often produce a more nuanced, resilient, and personally owned testimony — one that can weather life's storms.
3.scenarioDoneTo: Looking back on your faith journey, you realize the hardest questions you've wrestled with — about church history, science and religion, or the nature of God — have actually produced the deepest parts of your testimony.
3.scenarioYouDo: Looking back on your faith journey, you realize the hardest questions you've wrestled with — about church history, science and religion, or the nature of God — have actually produced the deepest parts of your testimony.

challenge.scenario: Looking back on your faith journey, you realize the hardest questions you've wrestled with — about church history, science and religion, or the nature of God — have actually produced the deepest parts of your testimony.
options: That's just a coincidence — real testimonies come only from spiritual experiences, not intellectual struggle
Since questions helped you, everyone must wrestle with your exact same issues to grow
Tell others to avoid hard questions entirely because your experience is the exception
Acknowledge that honest inquiry, when combined with faith, produces a tested and resilient testimony that becomes truly your own

3.tip: The strongest testimonies aren't the ones that were never challenged — they're the ones refined in the furnace of honest inquiry. (Doctrine and Covenants 6:36)

1.definition: Not everything you read or hear about the church is true — some sources are better than others.
1.scenarioDoneTo: A kid at school says something about your church that sounds really mean and wrong. You're not sure whether to believe them.
1.scenarioYouDo: A kid at school says something about your church that sounds really mean and wrong. You're not sure whether to believe them.

challenge.scenario: A kid at school says something about your church that sounds really mean and wrong. You're not sure whether to believe them.
options: Believe everything people say about the church without checking
Just stop going to church because of what one person said
Check with your parents, teachers, or the scriptures to find out what's actually true

1.tip: Not everything people say is true — always check with sources you trust. (Moroni 7:15-16)

2.definition: Not all sources of information about the gospel are equally reliable — learning to evaluate them is an important skill.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You find a website with shocking claims about the church. It looks professional and has lots of footnotes, but something feels off. Your seminary teacher reminds you to consider the source.
2.scenarioYouDo: You find a website with shocking claims about the church. It looks professional and has lots of footnotes, but something feels off. Your seminary teacher reminds you to consider the source.

challenge.scenario: You find a website with shocking claims about the church. It looks professional and has lots of footnotes, but something feels off. Your seminary teacher reminds you to consider the source.
options: Evaluate the source's intent, accuracy, and whether it tells the full story — not just how polished it looks
If it has footnotes, it must be completely true
Professional-looking websites are always reliable sources
Only trust sources that make you feel comfortable

2.tip: A polished presentation doesn't guarantee accuracy. Look deeper before you believe. (Doctrine and Covenants 46:7)

3.definition: Evaluating information about the church requires critical thinking about source reliability, author intent, methodology, and whether conclusions are honestly drawn from complete evidence.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A viral social media post claims to expose a 'hidden' church teaching, citing real documents but presenting them without any historical context. It has millions of views and thousands of comments agreeing.
3.scenarioYouDo: A viral social media post claims to expose a 'hidden' church teaching, citing real documents but presenting them without any historical context. It has millions of views and thousands of comments agreeing.

challenge.scenario: A viral social media post claims to expose a 'hidden' church teaching, citing real documents but presenting them without any historical context. It has millions of views and thousands of comments agreeing.
options: Millions of views and agreement mean it must be true
Viral popularity is irrelevant to truth claims — evaluate the sourcing, context, and intellectual honesty before forming conclusions
Ignore it completely — anything critical is automatically false
Share it immediately because people deserve to see 'the truth'

3.tip: Popularity is not a measure of truth. Viral content demands the same critical evaluation as any other source. (2 Nephi 9:28-29)

1.definition: The best place to learn about the gospel is from the scriptures, prophets, and your church leaders.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You want to know what the church teaches about Jesus. Your friend says to just look it up on any website, but your teacher says to start with the scriptures.
1.scenarioYouDo: You want to know what the church teaches about Jesus. Your friend says to just look it up on any website, but your teacher says to start with the scriptures.

challenge.scenario: You want to know what the church teaches about Jesus. Your friend says to just look it up on any website, but your teacher says to start with the scriptures.
options: Start with the scriptures and church materials — they come straight from the source
A random website is just as good as the scriptures for learning gospel truth
Don't bother learning about it at all

1.tip: When you want to know what the church teaches, go straight to the source — the scriptures and prophets! (2 Nephi 32:3)

2.definition: Primary sources — scriptures, prophets, and official church materials — should be your starting point for understanding what the church actually teaches.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A classmate claims the church believes something that sounds strange and wrong. Instead of just taking their word for it, you want to verify it yourself.
2.scenarioYouDo: A classmate claims the church believes something that sounds strange and wrong. Instead of just taking their word for it, you want to verify it yourself.

challenge.scenario: A classmate claims the church believes something that sounds strange and wrong. Instead of just taking their word for it, you want to verify it yourself.
options: Take their word for it — they probably know what they're talking about
Ask other classmates to see if they agree with the claim
Look up a meme about it for a quick answer
Check official church resources, scriptures, or conference talks to see what the church actually teaches

2.tip: Always check the original source before believing secondhand claims about what the church teaches. (Amos 3:7)

3.definition: When evaluating claims about church teachings or history, primary sources — actual documents, original texts, and official statements — are far more reliable than secondhand summaries.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A podcast confidently claims the church changed a specific doctrine, citing a blog post that summarizes a 19th-century document. You haven't read the original document yourself.
3.scenarioYouDo: A podcast confidently claims the church changed a specific doctrine, citing a blog post that summarizes a 19th-century document. You haven't read the original document yourself.

challenge.scenario: A podcast confidently claims the church changed a specific doctrine, citing a blog post that summarizes a 19th-century document. You haven't read the original document yourself.
options: Read the original document in context, compare it with official church resources and credible historical analysis, then draw your own conclusions
Trust the podcast — they sounded very confident and knowledgeable about it
Assume the church is hiding something because the podcast said so
Dismiss the podcast entirely without investigating, because podcasts are never reliable

3.tip: Never let someone else's summary replace your own careful reading of the original source. (Doctrine and Covenants 88:118)

1.definition: Ask yourself: is this person trying to help me learn, or are they just trying to make me upset?
1.scenarioDoneTo: Someone at school keeps saying mean things about your church and laughing. They are not asking real questions — they just want to bother you.
1.scenarioYouDo: Someone at school keeps saying mean things about your church and laughing. They are not asking real questions — they just want to bother you.

challenge.scenario: Someone at school keeps saying mean things about your church and laughing. They are not asking real questions — they just want to bother you.
options: They're trying to help you learn new things
They're trying to be mean, not helpful — you don't have to listen to people who mock your beliefs
You should always listen to everyone no matter how they treat you

1.tip: Pay attention to whether someone wants to help you or hurt you — it makes a big difference. (Moroni 7:17)

2.definition: The intent of an author or speaker matters — are they trying to help you understand, or trying to destroy your faith?
2.scenarioDoneTo: You find two articles about the same church history topic. One carefully explores the issue and invites reflection. The other uses mocking language and seems designed to shock and anger readers.
2.scenarioYouDo: You find two articles about the same church history topic. One carefully explores the issue and invites reflection. The other uses mocking language and seems designed to shock and anger readers.

challenge.scenario: You find two articles about the same church history topic. One carefully explores the issue and invites reflection. The other uses mocking language and seems designed to shock and anger readers.
options: Both articles are equally reliable because they cover the same topic
The one with the strongest, most shocking language is probably the most honest
Consider the author's intent — the one designed to shock and mock is less likely to be fair or accurate
Ignore both because you shouldn't read about church history at all

2.tip: The way something is written tells you a lot about why it was written. (Moroni 7:15-16)

3.definition: Evaluating an author's intent and framing is essential — a source designed to destroy faith will select, frame, and present facts very differently than one designed to honestly inform.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You encounter two presentations of the same historical event. One from a faith-positive historian who acknowledges complexity while maintaining respect. Another from an activist openly dedicated to pulling people away from the church. Both cite real sources.
3.scenarioYouDo: You encounter two presentations of the same historical event. One from a faith-positive historian who acknowledges complexity while maintaining respect. Another from an activist openly dedicated to pulling people away from the church. Both cite real sources.

challenge.scenario: You encounter two presentations of the same historical event. One from a faith-positive historian who acknowledges complexity while maintaining respect. Another from an activist openly dedicated to pulling people away from the church. Both cite real sources.
options: Both are equally trustworthy because they cite the same basic facts
Facts speak for themselves regardless of who presents them — framing never affects meaning
Only read the faith-positive source and ignore the critical one completely
Recognize that intent shapes framing — engage both critically, but understand that a source designed to destroy faith may selectively present evidence to support that goal

3.tip: Two people can use the same facts and tell very different stories. Always ask: what is this person trying to accomplish? (3 Nephi 14:15-20)

1.definition: Things look very different when you know the whole story — context helps you understand.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Someone tells you a prophet once said something that sounds strange. But when your dad reads you the full talk, it actually makes a lot more sense in context.
1.scenarioYouDo: Someone tells you a prophet once said something that sounds strange. But when your dad reads you the full talk, it actually makes a lot more sense in context.

challenge.scenario: Someone tells you a prophet once said something that sounds strange. But when your dad reads you the full talk, it actually makes a lot more sense in context.
options: One sentence out of a talk is enough to understand what someone meant
The full talk was confusing and didn't help at all
Hearing the full context helps you understand what was really being said

1.tip: Always try to get the whole story before you decide what something means. (Proverbs 18:13)

2.definition: Historical events and statements can look very different when you understand the full context — time period, culture, and circumstances all matter.
2.scenarioDoneTo: Someone shares an old quote from an early church leader that sounds offensive by today's standards. When you research the era, you discover it was a common view at the time and the leader actually held more progressive views than most of his peers.
2.scenarioYouDo: Someone shares an old quote from an early church leader that sounds offensive by today's standards. When you research the era, you discover it was a common view at the time and the leader actually held more progressive views than most of his peers.

challenge.scenario: Someone shares an old quote from an early church leader that sounds offensive by today's standards. When you research the era, you discover it was a common view at the time and the leader actually held more progressive views than most of his peers.
options: Understanding historical context helps you evaluate statements more fairly and accurately
Context doesn't matter — if it sounds bad today, it was bad then, period
Judge everything only by modern standards and ignore history entirely
Old quotes never matter because we've moved past that era already

2.tip: Pulling quotes out of their time period is like watching one scene of a movie and thinking you've seen the whole thing. (Ecclesiastes 3:1)

3.definition: Responsible evaluation of historical claims requires understanding the historical, cultural, linguistic, and theological context — stripping context away almost always distorts meaning.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A critical essay cites a journal entry from Joseph Smith to argue a controversial point, but you notice the essay omits the sentences before and after the quote, ignores the historical circumstances, and disregards contemporary scholarly analysis that interprets it differently.
3.scenarioYouDo: A critical essay cites a journal entry from Joseph Smith to argue a controversial point, but you notice the essay omits the sentences before and after the quote, ignores the historical circumstances, and disregards contemporary scholarly analysis that interprets it differently.

challenge.scenario: A critical essay cites a journal entry from Joseph Smith to argue a controversial point, but you notice the essay omits the sentences before and after the quote, ignores the historical circumstances, and disregards contemporary scholarly analysis that interprets it differently.
options: The essay is still completely valid because it used a real, verifiable quote
Context never changes the plain meaning of words on a page
Recognize that selectively quoting while stripping context is a well-known manipulation technique — responsible scholarship requires engaging the full picture
Dismiss the concern entirely because defending the church is more important than historical accuracy

3.tip: A quote without context is like a puzzle piece without the puzzle — it can be made to fit any picture. (2 Nephi 9:28)

1.definition: A half-truth is when someone tells you part of the truth but leaves out important parts to trick you.
1.scenarioDoneTo: A classmate says, 'Your church wouldn't let some people have the priesthood — that's so mean!' They didn't mention that the church changed the policy and leaders have spoken against past mistakes.
1.scenarioYouDo: A classmate says, 'Your church wouldn't let some people have the priesthood — that's so mean!' They didn't mention that the church changed the policy and leaders have spoken against past mistakes.

challenge.scenario: A classmate says, 'Your church wouldn't let some people have the priesthood — that's so mean!' They didn't mention that the church changed the policy and leaders have spoken against past mistakes.
options: That's a half-truth — they left out important parts of the story
Your classmate told the whole story and you should be really upset
Half of a story is always enough to understand the full picture

1.tip: Half the truth can be just as misleading as a lie. Always look for the whole picture. (John 8:32)

2.definition: Half-truths — true facts presented without full context or alongside misleading conclusions — can be more dangerous than outright lies because they feel believable.
2.scenarioDoneTo: An anti-church website lists several facts about church history that are technically true but presents them in the most negative light possible, leaving out all context, nuance, and faithful responses.
2.scenarioYouDo: An anti-church website lists several facts about church history that are technically true but presents them in the most negative light possible, leaving out all context, nuance, and faithful responses.

challenge.scenario: An anti-church website lists several facts about church history that are technically true but presents them in the most negative light possible, leaving out all context, nuance, and faithful responses.
options: If the individual facts are true, the overall conclusions must be true too
Half-truths are impossible — something is either completely true or completely false
Share the website with everyone because people deserve to know these facts
Recognize that true facts can be arranged to create a misleading narrative — always look for what's being left out

2.tip: The most effective deception uses real ingredients. Always ask: what's being left out? (2 Corinthians 11:14)

3.definition: Sophisticated misinformation often relies on technically accurate details arranged in misleading frameworks — recognizing this pattern is essential for navigating faith challenges honestly.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A documentary about the church uses verified historical documents but edits them with dramatic music, troubled narration, and interviews only with former members, creating a powerfully negative impression — even though much of the raw data has faithful explanations.
3.scenarioYouDo: A documentary about the church uses verified historical documents but edits them with dramatic music, troubled narration, and interviews only with former members, creating a powerfully negative impression — even though much of the raw data has faithful explanations.

challenge.scenario: A documentary about the church uses verified historical documents but edits them with dramatic music, troubled narration, and interviews only with former members, creating a powerfully negative impression — even though much of the raw data has faithful explanations.
options: The documentary must be accurate because it uses real, verified documents
Recognize that editorial choices — what to include, exclude, emphasize, and frame — can make accurate data tell a misleading story
Documentaries are always fair and balanced because they're professionally produced
Refuse to watch any documentary about the church under any circumstances

3.tip: Presentation shapes perception. Real documents in a misleading framework can be just as dishonest as fabricated ones. (Jacob 7:4)

1.definition: The Holy Ghost can help you feel what is true — He speaks through warm, peaceful feelings inside you.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You read something scary about the church online and feel upset. But when you read the Book of Mormon before bed, you feel warm and peaceful inside.
1.scenarioYouDo: You read something scary about the church online and feel upset. But when you read the Book of Mormon before bed, you feel warm and peaceful inside.

challenge.scenario: You read something scary about the church online and feel upset. But when you read the Book of Mormon before bed, you feel warm and peaceful inside.
options: The scary feeling from the website means it must be right
The peaceful feeling when reading the Book of Mormon is the Spirit helping you feel truth
Feelings don't mean anything at all

1.tip: The Holy Ghost speaks with peace and warmth — that's one way you can know something is true. (Doctrine and Covenants 8:2)

2.definition: The Spirit is your most reliable guide for discerning truth — learn to recognize how He speaks to you.
2.scenarioDoneTo: After reading a confusing article about the church, you feel anxious and unsettled. You decide to pray and read your scriptures, and a calm, clear feeling of peace replaces the anxiety.
2.scenarioYouDo: After reading a confusing article about the church, you feel anxious and unsettled. You decide to pray and read your scriptures, and a calm, clear feeling of peace replaces the anxiety.

challenge.scenario: After reading a confusing article about the church, you feel anxious and unsettled. You decide to pray and read your scriptures, and a calm, clear feeling of peace replaces the anxiety.
options: The anxiety you felt means the article was more truthful than the scriptures
The Spirit brought you peace and clarity — learn to trust that as a witness of truth
The peaceful feeling is just your brain trying to avoid hard truths
Ignore all feelings entirely and only trust pure logic

2.tip: The fruits of the Spirit — peace, love, clarity — are real signals. Learn to trust them. (Galatians 5:22-23)

3.definition: While logic and evidence are important, the witness of the Holy Ghost is the ultimate confirmer of spiritual truth — cultivating sensitivity to the Spirit is essential in an age of information overload.
3.scenarioDoneTo: After extensive study of a controversial topic, you have strong intellectual arguments on both sides. You take the matter to the Lord in the temple and receive a clear, undeniable spiritual confirmation that strengthens your faith.
3.scenarioYouDo: After extensive study of a controversial topic, you have strong intellectual arguments on both sides. You take the matter to the Lord in the temple and receive a clear, undeniable spiritual confirmation that strengthens your faith.

challenge.scenario: After extensive study of a controversial topic, you have strong intellectual arguments on both sides. You take the matter to the Lord in the temple and receive a clear, undeniable spiritual confirmation that strengthens your faith.
options: Spiritual experiences are just emotions and shouldn't outweigh intellectual arguments
Dismiss the experience because it can't be measured or replicated in a lab
Spiritual confirmations override the need to ever study or think critically about anything
Accept that the witness of the Spirit is a legitimate and powerful form of knowledge that complements — not replaces — intellectual inquiry

3.tip: The Spirit isn't a replacement for thinking — it's a higher channel of knowing that works alongside your mind. (Moroni 10:5)

1.definition: Before you decide what's true, try to hear more than one side of the story.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your friend at school says something negative about the church. Before deciding if it's true, you ask your parent what they think about it.
1.scenarioYouDo: Your friend at school says something negative about the church. Before deciding if it's true, you ask your parent what they think about it.

challenge.scenario: Your friend at school says something negative about the church. Before deciding if it's true, you ask your parent what they think about it.
options: Just believe the very first thing you hear
Only listen to one side because it's faster and easier
It's smart to hear more than one perspective before you decide what's true

1.tip: The first story you hear isn't always the full story. Be smart and listen to more than one side! (Proverbs 18:17)

2.definition: Before forming conclusions about a gospel issue, seek multiple trustworthy perspectives — one voice rarely tells the complete story.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You watch a YouTube video that criticizes a church teaching. Before making up your mind, you also read a church essay on the topic, talk to your bishop, and study relevant scriptures.
2.scenarioYouDo: You watch a YouTube video that criticizes a church teaching. Before making up your mind, you also read a church essay on the topic, talk to your bishop, and study relevant scriptures.

challenge.scenario: You watch a YouTube video that criticizes a church teaching. Before making up your mind, you also read a church essay on the topic, talk to your bishop, and study relevant scriptures.
options: One YouTube video is more than enough to form a strong opinion
You're way overthinking it — just go with your gut reaction
Gathering multiple perspectives before drawing conclusions shows wisdom and intellectual honesty
Avoid the topic entirely because it's way too complex to understand

2.tip: Wisdom isn't found in a single source — it's found by seeking understanding from many trustworthy places. (Doctrine and Covenants 88:118)

3.definition: Intellectual and spiritual rigor requires engaging multiple informed perspectives — both faithful and critical — while using discernment to weigh them appropriately.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You're researching a complex church history topic for a paper. You read the church's Gospel Topics essay, two academic histories by faithful LDS scholars, and a critical analysis by a non-LDS historian. Each source emphasizes different aspects of the event.
3.scenarioYouDo: You're researching a complex church history topic for a paper. You read the church's Gospel Topics essay, two academic histories by faithful LDS scholars, and a critical analysis by a non-LDS historian. Each source emphasizes different aspects of the event.

challenge.scenario: You're researching a complex church history topic for a paper. You read the church's Gospel Topics essay, two academic histories by faithful LDS scholars, and a critical analysis by a non-LDS historian. Each source emphasizes different aspects of the event.
options: Engaging multiple scholarly perspectives gives you a more complete picture — weigh each source's evidence, methodology, and potential biases thoughtfully
Just pick the source that confirms what you already believe and use that one
Only the official church essay matters — ignore all other historians entirely
The critical source is automatically more honest because it's not affiliated with the church

3.tip: The most honest conclusions come from engaging the best arguments on every side — not just the ones you're comfortable with. (Doctrine and Covenants 109:7)

1.definition: Just because someone is really smart at one thing doesn't mean they know everything about God.
1.scenarioDoneTo: A kid at school says, 'My dad is a scientist and he says God isn't real.' Scientists are really smart, so you feel confused.
1.scenarioYouDo: A kid at school says, 'My dad is a scientist and he says God isn't real.' Scientists are really smart, so you feel confused.

challenge.scenario: A kid at school says, 'My dad is a scientist and he says God isn't real.' Scientists are really smart, so you feel confused.
options: Being smart about science doesn't mean you know everything about God — spiritual things are learned in a special way
Scientists know everything about every subject, so God must not be real
Only scientists can tell you what's true about anything

1.tip: Being smart about one thing doesn't make you an expert on everything — especially spiritual things. (1 Corinthians 2:14)

2.definition: Academic credentials don't automatically make someone right about spiritual matters — spiritual truth requires spiritual methods of knowing.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A professor writes a book criticizing the Book of Mormon. He has a PhD and many publications. A seminary teacher with no PhD shares a testimony of the Book of Mormon. Your friend says the professor must be right because of his credentials.
2.scenarioYouDo: A professor writes a book criticizing the Book of Mormon. He has a PhD and many publications. A seminary teacher with no PhD shares a testimony of the Book of Mormon. Your friend says the professor must be right because of his credentials.

challenge.scenario: A professor writes a book criticizing the Book of Mormon. He has a PhD and many publications. A seminary teacher with no PhD shares a testimony of the Book of Mormon. Your friend says the professor must be right because of his credentials.
options: Only listen to people with PhDs about anything important in life
The seminary teacher must be wrong because they don't have a PhD
The professor must be right because he has a PhD and publications
Academic expertise in one field doesn't automatically translate to spiritual truth — evaluate arguments on their merits, not just the author's resume

2.tip: Credentials deserve respect, but they don't automatically make someone right about spiritual things. (2 Nephi 9:28-29)

3.definition: While academic expertise is valuable, it operates in a different domain than spiritual knowledge — conflating the two leads to errors on both sides.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A renowned historian writes a meticulously researched book concluding Joseph Smith fabricated the Book of Mormon. The research is thorough within secular methodology but excludes the possibility of divine involvement. A faithful LDS scholar with similar credentials reaches different conclusions using both secular and spiritual methodology.
3.scenarioYouDo: A renowned historian writes a meticulously researched book concluding Joseph Smith fabricated the Book of Mormon. The research is thorough within secular methodology but excludes the possibility of divine involvement. A faithful LDS scholar with similar credentials reaches different conclusions using both secular and spiritual methodology.

challenge.scenario: A renowned historian writes a meticulously researched book concluding Joseph Smith fabricated the Book of Mormon. The research is thorough within secular methodology but excludes the possibility of divine involvement. A faithful LDS scholar with similar credentials reaches different conclusions using both secular and spiritual methodology.
options: The secular historian must be right because they're more objective by definition
Academic research is completely irrelevant to faith — only prayer matters
Both scholars bring valuable expertise, but dismissing either approach leaves you with an incomplete picture — weigh each source's evidence, methodology, and assumptions thoughtfully
The faithful scholar can't be trusted because faith automatically biases all of their work

3.tip: Smart people can reach wrong conclusions by starting with wrong assumptions. Evaluate methods and evidence, not just titles. (1 Corinthians 1:25)

1.definition: You can judge a source by its results — does it bring you closer to God or push you away?
1.scenarioDoneTo: You read two things about the church. One makes you feel hopeful and want to pray. The other makes you feel angry and want to give up on everything.
1.scenarioYouDo: You read two things about the church. One makes you feel hopeful and want to pray. The other makes you feel angry and want to give up on everything.

challenge.scenario: You read two things about the church. One makes you feel hopeful and want to pray. The other makes you feel angry and want to give up on everything.
options: The angry feeling means that source was probably more interesting and useful
The one that brings you closer to God and inspires good feelings is bearing good fruit
Pick whichever source was longer because longer is usually better

1.tip: Good fruit means a good tree. If something brings you closer to God, that's a really good sign! (Matthew 7:16-20)

2.definition: Judge information sources by their fruits — does engaging with them produce faith, hope, and a desire to be better, or bitterness, cynicism, and despair?
2.scenarioDoneTo: After spending time on a particular website, you notice you feel more cynical, less interested in prayer, and more critical of everyone. A different resource about the same topics leaves you feeling peaceful and thoughtful.
2.scenarioYouDo: After spending time on a particular website, you notice you feel more cynical, less interested in prayer, and more critical of everyone. A different resource about the same topics leaves you feeling peaceful and thoughtful.

challenge.scenario: After spending time on a particular website, you notice you feel more cynical, less interested in prayer, and more critical of everyone. A different resource about the same topics leaves you feeling peaceful and thoughtful.
options: The fruits of what you consume matter — bitterness and cynicism are warning signs that a source may not be leading you toward truth
The one that makes you cynical is probably more honest because truth always hurts
Feeling bad after reading something always means you're learning something really important
Both sources are equally fine — how you feel while reading is completely irrelevant

2.tip: Truth can challenge you, but it shouldn't poison you. Watch the fruits in your own life. (Moroni 7:13)

3.definition: The 'by their fruits' test is a legitimate and powerful evaluation tool — examine what consistent engagement with a source produces in your life, relationships, and spiritual well-being over time.
3.scenarioDoneTo: Over several months, you notice that friends who consistently consume content from a particular ex-member influencer have become increasingly bitter, dismissive of other viewpoints, and isolated — while a friend who engaged with challenging questions through faithful resources grew more empathetic, thoughtful, and spiritually grounded.
3.scenarioYouDo: Over several months, you notice that friends who consistently consume content from a particular ex-member influencer have become increasingly bitter, dismissive of other viewpoints, and isolated — while a friend who engaged with challenging questions through faithful resources grew more empathetic, thoughtful, and spiritually grounded.

challenge.scenario: Over several months, you notice that friends who consistently consume content from a particular ex-member influencer have become increasingly bitter, dismissive of other viewpoints, and isolated — while a friend who engaged with challenging questions through faithful resources grew more empathetic, thoughtful, and spiritually grounded.
options: Observable patterns in people's lives tell you nothing about the sources they consume
Recognize that the long-term fruits of a source — in your life and others' — are a powerful and legitimate tool for evaluating its reliability and influence
Bitterness and isolation are signs of someone being more 'awake' and intellectually honest
People's spiritual outcomes are completely unrelated to what they read and watch

3.tip: Time reveals truth. Watch where a source leads people over months and years, not just what it claims in one article. (Matthew 7:16-20)

1.definition: Good sources try to build your faith, not tear it down. Helpful people want to bring you closer to God.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Someone gives you a pamphlet outside the temple that says bad things about your church. They don't seem to care about you — they just want you to stop believing.
1.scenarioYouDo: Someone gives you a pamphlet outside the temple that says bad things about your church. They don't seem to care about you — they just want you to stop believing.

challenge.scenario: Someone gives you a pamphlet outside the temple that says bad things about your church. They don't seem to care about you — they just want you to stop believing.
options: The pamphlet must be true because it was on printed paper
Read the pamphlet because all information is always equally valuable
Notice that the person isn't trying to help you — they're just trying to tear down your faith

1.tip: If someone's goal is to tear things down instead of build things up, that tells you something about their real intent. (Moroni 7:17)

2.definition: There's a meaningful difference between sources that challenge you to think deeper and sources that just want to tear faith apart — learn to tell them apart.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You find two podcasts about church issues. One honestly discusses tough topics while encouraging listeners to keep seeking God. The other mocks believers, uses sarcasm, and celebrates when people leave the church.
2.scenarioYouDo: You find two podcasts about church issues. One honestly discusses tough topics while encouraging listeners to keep seeking God. The other mocks believers, uses sarcasm, and celebrates when people leave the church.

challenge.scenario: You find two podcasts about church issues. One honestly discusses tough topics while encouraging listeners to keep seeking God. The other mocks believers, uses sarcasm, and celebrates when people leave the church.
options: Both podcasts are the same because they discuss identical topics
Choose sources that help you think honestly AND build faith — mockery and celebration of people losing faith are red flags
The mocking podcast is more honest because it's more blunt and direct
Avoid all podcasts about faith because they're all equally harmful

2.tip: The tone tells the tale. Sources that mock and celebrate faith loss have a very different purpose than ones that honestly inform. (Ephesians 4:29)

3.definition: Discerning between constructive challenge and destructive intent is crucial — the best sources can address the hardest topics while still pointing toward truth, faith, and God.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You follow a social media account run by a former member who frames every post about the church as a victory against harmful religion. Separately, you read a blog by a faithful scholar who candidly discusses the same difficult issues while sharing how they've navigated them within their faith.
3.scenarioYouDo: You follow a social media account run by a former member who frames every post about the church as a victory against harmful religion. Separately, you read a blog by a faithful scholar who candidly discusses the same difficult issues while sharing how they've navigated them within their faith.

challenge.scenario: You follow a social media account run by a former member who frames every post about the church as a victory against harmful religion. Separately, you read a blog by a faithful scholar who candidly discusses the same difficult issues while sharing how they've navigated them within their faith.
options: The former member is automatically more trustworthy because they've 'seen both sides'
The faithful scholar is automatically biased and therefore can't be trusted about anything
You should only ever read content from faithful sources and never engage with criticism at all
Both people engage real issues, but the source whose consistent purpose is destroying faith should be weighed differently than one whose purpose is navigating hard truths while remaining faithful

3.tip: Addressing hard truths and destroying faith aren't the same thing. The best sources do the first without the second. (Moroni 7:16-17)

1.definition: Asking questions about the gospel is a good thing — it helps you learn and grow.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your little brother says, 'I have a question about something in the scriptures.' Your mom smiles and says that's great. Why is asking questions good?
1.scenarioYouDo: Your little brother says, 'I have a question about something in the scriptures.' Your mom smiles and says that's great. Why is asking questions good?

challenge.scenario: Your little brother says, 'I have a question about something in the scriptures.' Your mom smiles and says that's great. Why is asking questions good?
options: Stop thinking about it entirely
Keep asking questions and look for answers
Just agree with whatever anyone says

1.tip: Questions help us learn — Heavenly Father loves when we come to Him with questions. (James 1:5)

2.definition: Doubt gives up, but questioning seeks answers with faith.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A friend at school says, 'I heard something weird about the Church and now I don't know what to believe.' They're thinking about skipping church altogether. What would you suggest?
2.scenarioYouDo: A friend at school says, 'I heard something weird about the Church and now I don't know what to believe.' They're thinking about skipping church altogether. What would you suggest?

challenge.scenario: A friend at school says, 'I heard something weird about the Church and now I don't know what to believe.' They're thinking about skipping church altogether. What would you suggest?
options: Ignore the question and hope it goes away
Decide the Church must be wrong
Research your question and pray to find an honest answer
Tell no one about your questions

2.tip: Having questions is normal — what matters is whether you seek answers with a sincere heart. (Alma 32:21)

3.definition: Sincere questioning is an act of faith; cynical doubt is a decision to stop seeking.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A college roommate tells you, 'I've been reading a lot of critical material about the Church and I'm starting to think it's all made up.' You notice they've stopped praying and attending church. How do you respond thoughtfully?
3.scenarioYouDo: A college roommate tells you, 'I've been reading a lot of critical material about the Church and I'm starting to think it's all made up.' You notice they've stopped praying and attending church. How do you respond thoughtfully?

challenge.scenario: A college roommate tells you, 'I've been reading a lot of critical material about the Church and I'm starting to think it's all made up.' You notice they've stopped praying and attending church. How do you respond thoughtfully?
options: Recognize that sincere questioning is an act of faith, while cynical doubt seeks to destroy faith — encourage them to keep seeking
Assume that if they have questions, their testimony must be weak
Decide that any doubt means the Church isn't true
Avoid all sources of information to protect your beliefs

3.tip: There's a crucial difference between earnestly seeking truth and surrendering to cynicism — one builds faith, the other dismantles it. (Ether 12:6)

1.definition: When you feel confused, remember what you DO know is true.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You're feeling confused about something your teacher said about God. But you remember how you felt when you prayed last night — warm and peaceful. What should you do?
1.scenarioYouDo: You're feeling confused about something your teacher said about God. But you remember how you felt when you prayed last night — warm and peaceful. What should you do?

challenge.scenario: You're feeling confused about something your teacher said about God. But you remember how you felt when you prayed last night — warm and peaceful. What should you do?
options: Remember what you do know is true and hold on tight
Forget everything you ever learned
Wait until you understand everything perfectly

1.tip: Even when some things feel confusing, hold onto the good things you already know. (Helaman 5:12)

2.definition: One unanswered question doesn't erase everything you've already felt and learned.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You come across a social media post criticizing the Church and it makes you uncomfortable. You start wondering if anything you've been taught is true. But you remember feeling the Spirit strongly at youth conference last summer. What do you do?
2.scenarioYouDo: You come across a social media post criticizing the Church and it makes you uncomfortable. You start wondering if anything you've been taught is true. But you remember feeling the Spirit strongly at youth conference last summer. What do you do?

challenge.scenario: You come across a social media post criticizing the Church and it makes you uncomfortable. You start wondering if anything you've been taught is true. But you remember feeling the Spirit strongly at youth conference last summer. What do you do?
options: Throw away your whole testimony over one hard question
Focus on the truths you do know while working through the unknowns
Pretend you have no questions at all
Decide you can't believe anything until you know everything

2.tip: One hard question doesn't cancel out years of spiritual experiences — press forward with what you know. (2 Nephi 31:20)

3.definition: Anchor yourself in confirmed spiritual experiences while patiently working through unresolved questions.
3.scenarioDoneTo: During your mission prep, you discover a historical issue that genuinely bothers you. You've had powerful spiritual confirmations of the Book of Mormon's truthfulness, but this issue creates real dissonance. How do you navigate this responsibly?
3.scenarioYouDo: During your mission prep, you discover a historical issue that genuinely bothers you. You've had powerful spiritual confirmations of the Book of Mormon's truthfulness, but this issue creates real dissonance. How do you navigate this responsibly?

challenge.scenario: During your mission prep, you discover a historical issue that genuinely bothers you. You've had powerful spiritual confirmations of the Book of Mormon's truthfulness, but this issue creates real dissonance. How do you navigate this responsibly?
options: Abandon all belief until every question is resolved
Suppress your questions and refuse to think about them
Demand perfect knowledge before committing to any belief
Anchor yourself in confirmed spiritual experiences while patiently studying unresolved questions

3.tip: Your spiritual experiences are real data points — don't discard them because of unresolved questions. Hold on and keep seeking. (D&C 6:36)

1.definition: Give your faith a chance before giving up.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You feel worried that maybe Heavenly Father didn't hear your prayer last night. A friend says, 'Maybe prayer doesn't work.' What should you try first?
1.scenarioYouDo: You feel worried that maybe Heavenly Father didn't hear your prayer last night. A friend says, 'Maybe prayer doesn't work.' What should you try first?

challenge.scenario: You feel worried that maybe Heavenly Father didn't hear your prayer last night. A friend says, 'Maybe prayer doesn't work.' What should you try first?
options: Believe every worry that comes to mind
Stop believing in anything
Give your faith a chance before giving up

1.tip: Don't let one worried feeling make you forget all the good times you've felt close to Heavenly Father. (Proverbs 3:5)

2.definition: Question your doubts just as much as you question your faith.
2.scenarioDoneTo: After reading something critical online, you start doubting your testimony. But you realize you've never questioned whether the critical source is reliable. What's the wisest approach?
2.scenarioYouDo: After reading something critical online, you start doubting your testimony. But you realize you've never questioned whether the critical source is reliable. What's the wisest approach?

challenge.scenario: After reading something critical online, you start doubting your testimony. But you realize you've never questioned whether the critical source is reliable. What's the wisest approach?
options: Question your doubts just as much as you question your faith
Trust every negative thing you read about the Church
Only listen to people who criticize the Church
Assume doubts always come from the Holy Ghost

2.tip: President Uchtdorf said, 'Doubt your doubts before you doubt your faith.' Apply the same standard of proof to both sides. (Alma 32:27)
