3.definition: Apply the same intellectual rigor to your doubts that you apply to your beliefs.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A friend who has left the Church says you only believe because you were raised in it. You realize they accepted critical arguments without much scrutiny. How do you think about this fairly?
3.scenarioYouDo: A friend who has left the Church says you only believe because you were raised in it. You realize they accepted critical arguments without much scrutiny. How do you think about this fairly?

challenge.scenario: A friend who has left the Church says you only believe because you were raised in it. You realize they accepted critical arguments without much scrutiny. How do you think about this fairly?
options: Accept every critical claim about the Church at face value
Assume that doubt is the natural result of gaining more knowledge
Apply the same scrutiny to your doubts that you apply to your beliefs — examine sources, motives, and evidence on all sides
Ignore all evidence that supports your faith

3.tip: Honest seeking demands equal scrutiny — test your doubts with the same rigor you use to test your faith. (Moroni 7:16)

1.definition: Your testimony grows little by little, like a plant — not all at once.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your friend says she knows EVERYTHING about the gospel. You feel bad because you're still learning. What's the truth?
1.scenarioYouDo: Your friend says she knows EVERYTHING about the gospel. You feel bad because you're still learning. What's the truth?

challenge.scenario: Your friend says she knows EVERYTHING about the gospel. You feel bad because you're still learning. What's the truth?
options: You should know everything about the gospel right now
Your testimony grows little by little over time
Only grown-ups can have testimonies

1.tip: Heavenly Father teaches us line upon line — nobody learns it all at once, and that's perfectly okay. (2 Nephi 28:30)

2.definition: Testimonies grow gradually through consistent effort, not overnight.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You feel like other kids in your ward have stronger testimonies than you. You've been reading the Book of Mormon but haven't had a dramatic spiritual experience yet. What should you remember?
2.scenarioYouDo: You feel like other kids in your ward have stronger testimonies than you. You've been reading the Book of Mormon but haven't had a dramatic spiritual experience yet. What should you remember?

challenge.scenario: You feel like other kids in your ward have stronger testimonies than you. You've been reading the Book of Mormon but haven't had a dramatic spiritual experience yet. What should you remember?
options: If your testimony isn't as strong as your bishop's, give up
You should feel the Spirit perfectly every single day
Real testimonies appear overnight and never change
Testimonies grow gradually through consistent scripture study, prayer, and patience

2.tip: A testimony is like a sunrise — it grows brighter gradually. Keep doing the small, faithful things every day. (D&C 50:24)

3.definition: Testimony development is a lifelong process of incremental revelation and trust in God's timing.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You're preparing to leave for college and feel frustrated that your testimony doesn't feel as rock-solid as you think it should. You've been faithful but still have unresolved questions. How do you view this?
3.scenarioYouDo: You're preparing to leave for college and feel frustrated that your testimony doesn't feel as rock-solid as you think it should. You've been faithful but still have unresolved questions. How do you view this?

challenge.scenario: You're preparing to leave for college and feel frustrated that your testimony doesn't feel as rock-solid as you think it should. You've been faithful but still have unresolved questions. How do you view this?
options: Spiritual maturity should come immediately after baptism and anything less is failure
Testimony development is a lifelong process of incremental revelation, patience, and trust in God's timing — even prophets grew in their understanding over years
If you haven't gained a perfect testimony by age 18, something is fundamentally wrong
Only people with perfect testimonies should attend church

3.tip: Even the prophets grew 'line upon line.' A testimony in progress is still a testimony — keep building. (Isaiah 28:10)

1.definition: Keep praying and reading scriptures even when it feels hard.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You're feeling confused about a gospel topic and don't feel like praying tonight. What should you do?
1.scenarioYouDo: You're feeling confused about a gospel topic and don't feel like praying tonight. What should you do?

challenge.scenario: You're feeling confused about a gospel topic and don't feel like praying tonight. What should you do?
options: Keep praying and reading scriptures even when it feels hard
Stop going to church until all your questions are answered
Only pray when you feel like it

1.tip: Praying when it's hard is like exercising when you're tired — it makes you stronger. (3 Nephi 18:20)

2.definition: Don't let doubt drive you away from the spiritual practices that bring peace.
2.scenarioDoneTo: Your friend says, 'I'm not going to church anymore until I figure out if I even believe.' You know they used to feel the Spirit at church. What do you say?
2.scenarioYouDo: Your friend says, 'I'm not going to church anymore until I figure out if I even believe.' You know they used to feel the Spirit at church. What do you say?

challenge.scenario: Your friend says, 'I'm not going to church anymore until I figure out if I even believe.' You know they used to feel the Spirit at church. What do you say?
options: Stop all spiritual habits when you have doubts
Wait until you feel 100% sure before you pray again
Continue faithful practices like prayer and church attendance while working through your doubts
Only rely on the internet for answers about the gospel

2.tip: Stopping spiritual practices during doubt is like quitting your medicine when you're sick — stay connected to what heals. (Moroni 10:4)

3.definition: Spiritual disciplines during doubt keep you connected to the source of answers.
3.scenarioDoneTo: During a faith crisis in your young adult years, you feel tempted to withdraw from church entirely. A mentor suggests that attending, praying, and serving — even while struggling — keeps the channel open for answers. How do you evaluate this advice?
3.scenarioYouDo: During a faith crisis in your young adult years, you feel tempted to withdraw from church entirely. A mentor suggests that attending, praying, and serving — even while struggling — keeps the channel open for answers. How do you evaluate this advice?

challenge.scenario: During a faith crisis in your young adult years, you feel tempted to withdraw from church entirely. A mentor suggests that attending, praying, and serving — even while struggling — keeps the channel open for answers. How do you evaluate this advice?
options: Withdraw from all church participation during periods of questioning
Replace spiritual practices with purely academic study of religion
Assume that spiritual experiences during doubt are just wishful thinking
Maintain spiritual disciplines during doubt because they keep you connected to the source of answers

3.tip: Don't disconnect from the very practices that position you to receive answers. Stay plugged in, even when it's hard. (D&C 88:63)

1.definition: It's okay to say 'I don't know yet' — that doesn't mean it's not true.
1.scenarioDoneTo: A friend asks you a hard question about God and you don't know the answer. You feel embarrassed. What's a good thing to say?
1.scenarioYouDo: A friend asks you a hard question about God and you don't know the answer. You feel embarrassed. What's a good thing to say?

challenge.scenario: A friend asks you a hard question about God and you don't know the answer. You feel embarrassed. What's a good thing to say?
options: You have to understand everything about God right now
If you can't explain something, it must not be true
It's okay to say 'I don't know yet' and keep learning

1.tip: Nephi said, 'I don't know the meaning of all things, but I know God loves His children.' That's a great answer! (1 Nephi 11:17)

2.definition: Saying 'I don't know yet' is honest and faithful — it means you're still seeking.
2.scenarioDoneTo: In seminary, someone asks a tough doctrinal question and the teacher says, 'That's a great question — I don't know the full answer yet, but let's study it together.' Some students think the teacher is weak. What do you think?
2.scenarioYouDo: In seminary, someone asks a tough doctrinal question and the teacher says, 'That's a great question — I don't know the full answer yet, but let's study it together.' Some students think the teacher is weak. What do you think?

challenge.scenario: In seminary, someone asks a tough doctrinal question and the teacher says, 'That's a great question — I don't know the full answer yet, but let's study it together.' Some students think the teacher is weak. What do you think?
options: Not knowing something means the Church is false
Saying 'I don't know yet' is honest and shows you're still seeking truth
You should make up an answer rather than admit you don't know
If you can't answer every question, you shouldn't share your testimony

2.tip: Honesty about what you don't know yet is a sign of integrity, not weakness. (Mosiah 4:9)

3.definition: Intellectual humility — acknowledging what you don't yet know — is a sign of faith, not weakness.
3.scenarioDoneTo: At a university religion class, a professor challenges students to explain a complex doctrinal issue. You don't have a complete answer. Some classmates leave the Church when they can't resolve every question immediately. How do you approach this differently?
3.scenarioYouDo: At a university religion class, a professor challenges students to explain a complex doctrinal issue. You don't have a complete answer. Some classmates leave the Church when they can't resolve every question immediately. How do you approach this differently?

challenge.scenario: At a university religion class, a professor challenges students to explain a complex doctrinal issue. You don't have a complete answer. Some classmates leave the Church when they can't resolve every question immediately. How do you approach this differently?
options: Intellectual humility — acknowledging what you don't yet know — is a sign of mature faith, not weakness
Admitting uncertainty means your testimony is crumbling
You must have a perfect answer to every gospel question before you can believe
Unanswered questions are proof that the Church can't be true

3.tip: Saying 'I don't know yet' requires more courage and honesty than pretending you have all the answers. (Proverbs 3:5-6)

1.definition: Talk to people you trust when you feel confused about the gospel.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You're feeling worried about something you heard about the Church at school. You don't know who to talk to. What's the best idea?
1.scenarioYouDo: You're feeling worried about something you heard about the Church at school. You don't know who to talk to. What's the best idea?

challenge.scenario: You're feeling worried about something you heard about the Church at school. You don't know who to talk to. What's the best idea?
options: Only talk to people who don't believe in God
Talk to someone you trust, like a parent or teacher, when you feel confused
Keep all your worries to yourself forever

1.tip: You don't have to figure everything out alone — trusted people can help you find answers. (Ecclesiastes 4:9-10)

2.definition: Surround yourself with faithful friends and mentors when you're struggling.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You've been experiencing doubt and a classmate invites you to an online group that only talks about reasons to leave the Church. Another friend invites you to talk with your youth leader about your questions. Which is more helpful?
2.scenarioYouDo: You've been experiencing doubt and a classmate invites you to an online group that only talks about reasons to leave the Church. Another friend invites you to talk with your youth leader about your questions. Which is more helpful?

challenge.scenario: You've been experiencing doubt and a classmate invites you to an online group that only talks about reasons to leave the Church. Another friend invites you to talk with your youth leader about your questions. Which is more helpful?
options: Seek out faithful friends, family, or leaders who can help you work through questions
Only discuss your doubts with strangers online
Hide your struggles so nobody knows
Only talk to people who have already left the Church

2.tip: Iron sharpens iron — faithful friends who take your questions seriously are worth their weight in gold. (Proverbs 27:17)

3.definition: Build a support network of faithful mentors who create safe space for honest questioning.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You're experiencing a genuine faith crisis and feel like no one at church would understand. Online communities of former members feel welcoming, but they consistently steer conversations toward leaving. How do you find balanced support?
3.scenarioYouDo: You're experiencing a genuine faith crisis and feel like no one at church would understand. Online communities of former members feel welcoming, but they consistently steer conversations toward leaving. How do you find balanced support?

challenge.scenario: You're experiencing a genuine faith crisis and feel like no one at church would understand. Online communities of former members feel welcoming, but they consistently steer conversations toward leaving. How do you find balanced support?
options: Only engage with communities that reinforce your doubts
Isolate yourself because your questions make you feel unworthy
Build a support network of faithful mentors and friends who create safe space for honest, rigorous questioning
Avoid anyone with a strong testimony because they won't understand you

3.tip: Find people who love the gospel AND take your questions seriously — you need both faith and intellectual honesty in your support system. (Galatians 6:2)

1.definition: Even Jesus's closest friends sometimes had questions and needed more faith.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You learn in Primary that Thomas, one of Jesus's apostles, didn't believe Jesus was alive again until he saw Him. What does that teach us?
1.scenarioYouDo: You learn in Primary that Thomas, one of Jesus's apostles, didn't believe Jesus was alive again until he saw Him. What does that teach us?

challenge.scenario: You learn in Primary that Thomas, one of Jesus's apostles, didn't believe Jesus was alive again until he saw Him. What does that teach us?
options: Even Jesus's apostles sometimes had questions and needed more faith
Only bad people ever doubt
The apostles never struggled with anything

1.tip: If even the apostles had moments of doubt, it's okay for you to have questions too. Keep believing! (John 20:27)

2.definition: Even great leaders in the scriptures wrestled with moments of doubt.
2.scenarioDoneTo: In seminary, you read about Peter walking on water and then sinking when he was afraid. You realize even Peter, the chief apostle, had a moment of doubt. How does this apply to you?
2.scenarioYouDo: In seminary, you read about Peter walking on water and then sinking when he was afraid. You realize even Peter, the chief apostle, had a moment of doubt. How does this apply to you?

challenge.scenario: In seminary, you read about Peter walking on water and then sinking when he was afraid. You realize even Peter, the chief apostle, had a moment of doubt. How does this apply to you?
options: If you have doubts, you're nothing like the prophets or apostles
Faithful people never experience uncertainty
Only people outside the Church deal with hard questions
Even Thomas, one of Jesus's own apostles, needed to see and touch the Savior to believe — doubt doesn't disqualify you from faith

2.tip: The father who said 'Lord, I believe; help thou mine unbelief' is one of the most honest prayers in scripture. You can say it too. (Mark 9:24)

3.definition: Scripture is filled with faithful people who wrestled with doubt and emerged with stronger faith.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A friend argues that doubt is a modern problem and that truly faithful people in the scriptures never struggled. You know this isn't historically accurate. How do you respond?
3.scenarioYouDo: A friend argues that doubt is a modern problem and that truly faithful people in the scriptures never struggled. You know this isn't historically accurate. How do you respond?

challenge.scenario: A friend argues that doubt is a modern problem and that truly faithful people in the scriptures never struggled. You know this isn't historically accurate. How do you respond?
options: Having doubts disqualifies you from spiritual leadership or service
Scripture is full of faithful people — Peter, Thomas, Joseph Smith — who wrestled with moments of doubt and emerged with stronger, more refined faith
Doubt is a modern phenomenon that ancient prophets never experienced
If prophets in the scriptures doubted, then faith itself must be unreliable

3.tip: The Bible and Book of Mormon are full of honest doubters who became some of God's greatest servants. Doubt can be a doorway to deeper faith. (Matthew 14:31)

1.definition: Satan wants you to feel alone and give up, but Heavenly Father wants you to keep trying.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You're feeling really down about a gospel question and feel like nobody understands you. A thought comes into your mind: 'Just give up — nobody at church cares about you.' What's really happening?
1.scenarioYouDo: You're feeling really down about a gospel question and feel like nobody understands you. A thought comes into your mind: 'Just give up — nobody at church cares about you.' What's really happening?

challenge.scenario: You're feeling really down about a gospel question and feel like nobody understands you. A thought comes into your mind: 'Just give up — nobody at church cares about you.' What's really happening?
options: Every thought you have comes from Satan
Questions about the gospel are always bad
Satan wants you to feel alone and give up, but Heavenly Father wants you to keep trying

1.tip: When you feel alone and hopeless, that's not from God. Heavenly Father always invites you to come closer, never to give up. (2 Nephi 2:27)

2.definition: Satan uses doubt as a tool to isolate you from the Church and your support system.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You notice that since you started doubting, you've pulled away from your church friends, stopped going to mutual, and spend most of your time reading critical content alone. A leader gently points this out. What do you realize?
2.scenarioYouDo: You notice that since you started doubting, you've pulled away from your church friends, stopped going to mutual, and spend most of your time reading critical content alone. A leader gently points this out. What do you realize?

challenge.scenario: You notice that since you started doubting, you've pulled away from your church friends, stopped going to mutual, and spend most of your time reading critical content alone. A leader gently points this out. What do you realize?
options: Any question about the Church is from the adversary
Satan often uses doubt to isolate you from the Church and the people who care about you
You should never think critically about your beliefs
Feeling confused always means you've sinned

2.tip: Watch for the pattern of isolation — the adversary wants to separate you from the people and practices that strengthen you. (Ephesians 6:11)

3.definition: The adversary's strategy is to make honest questions feel like reasons to leave rather than reasons to seek deeper understanding.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You've noticed a pattern: every time you encounter a difficult gospel question, the internal push is not toward study and prayer, but toward anger, isolation, and abandoning faith. A wise mentor says, 'Pay attention to where the doubt is leading you.' How do you evaluate this?
3.scenarioYouDo: You've noticed a pattern: every time you encounter a difficult gospel question, the internal push is not toward study and prayer, but toward anger, isolation, and abandoning faith. A wise mentor says, 'Pay attention to where the doubt is leading you.' How do you evaluate this?

challenge.scenario: You've noticed a pattern: every time you encounter a difficult gospel question, the internal push is not toward study and prayer, but toward anger, isolation, and abandoning faith. A wise mentor says, 'Pay attention to where the doubt is leading you.' How do you evaluate this?
options: All intellectual questions about the gospel originate from Satan
Critical thinking and faith are always incompatible
If you feel doubt, you must have done something wrong spiritually
Recognize that the adversary's strategy is to turn honest questions into feelings of isolation and hopelessness — evaluate whether your doubt is driving you toward truth or away from it

3.tip: Questions that lead you toward God are inspired. Feelings that isolate and embitter are not. Pay attention to the trajectory of your doubt. (2 Nephi 28:20)

1.definition: Keep praying and asking — Heavenly Father will help you find answers.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You prayed about something but didn't get an answer right away. You're thinking about giving up. What should you remember?
1.scenarioYouDo: You prayed about something but didn't get an answer right away. You're thinking about giving up. What should you remember?

challenge.scenario: You prayed about something but didn't get an answer right away. You're thinking about giving up. What should you remember?
options: Stop looking for answers after the first try
Keep praying and asking questions — Heavenly Father will help you find answers in His time
Only look for answers in places that criticize the Church

1.tip: Jesus said, 'Ask, and it shall be given you.' Sometimes answers take time, but keep asking! (Matthew 7:7)

2.definition: Persistent, faithful seeking leads to answers — don't give up too soon.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You've prayed several times about a concern and feel like you haven't gotten a clear answer. You're tempted to give up on prayer entirely. What's a better approach?
2.scenarioYouDo: You've prayed several times about a concern and feel like you haven't gotten a clear answer. You're tempted to give up on prayer entirely. What's a better approach?

challenge.scenario: You've prayed several times about a concern and feel like you haven't gotten a clear answer. You're tempted to give up on prayer entirely. What's a better approach?
options: Give up if you don't get an answer right away
Assume God doesn't care about your questions
Persistently seek answers through prayer, study, and counsel with trusted leaders
Decide after one prayer that God isn't listening

2.tip: Seek learning 'by study and also by faith' — combine your best thinking with your most heartfelt prayers. (D&C 88:118)

3.definition: Commit to lifelong faithful inquiry — combining prayer, study, and patience with God's timeline.
3.scenarioDoneTo: After months of studying a difficult Church history issue, you realize you may not fully resolve it anytime soon. You still feel the Spirit when you serve and worship. How do you proceed?
3.scenarioYouDo: After months of studying a difficult Church history issue, you realize you may not fully resolve it anytime soon. You still feel the Spirit when you serve and worship. How do you proceed?

challenge.scenario: After months of studying a difficult Church history issue, you realize you may not fully resolve it anytime soon. You still feel the Spirit when you serve and worship. How do you proceed?
options: Commit to a lifelong posture of faithful inquiry — combining earnest prayer, diligent study, and patience with God's timeline for revealing answers
Expect God to answer all questions instantly or conclude He doesn't exist
Stop studying once you've read a single critical article
Assume that unanswered prayers mean God has abandoned you

3.tip: Some of the most important questions in life take years to resolve. Faithful persistence — not instant resolution — is the mark of a seeking soul. (James 1:5)

1.definition: Prophets are real people who make mistakes, but God still calls them to lead.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You learn that a prophet in the scriptures made a mistake. Your friend says, 'Then he can't really be a prophet!' What do you think?
1.scenarioYouDo: You learn that a prophet in the scriptures made a mistake. Your friend says, 'Then he can't really be a prophet!' What do you think?

challenge.scenario: You learn that a prophet in the scriptures made a mistake. Your friend says, 'Then he can't really be a prophet!' What do you think?
options: Prophets are real people who make mistakes but are still called by God
Prophets are perfect and never make mistakes
If a prophet makes a mistake, he can't be a real prophet

1.tip: God picks regular people to be prophets — they're not perfect, but He helps them do His work. (D&C 1:24)

2.definition: Prophets are imperfect humans whom God calls and works through despite their weaknesses.
2.scenarioDoneTo: In seminary, you learn that Joseph Smith made some errors in judgment during his lifetime. A classmate says, 'If he wasn't perfect, how can we trust the revelations?' How do you respond?
2.scenarioYouDo: In seminary, you learn that Joseph Smith made some errors in judgment during his lifetime. A classmate says, 'If he wasn't perfect, how can we trust the revelations?' How do you respond?

challenge.scenario: In seminary, you learn that Joseph Smith made some errors in judgment during his lifetime. A classmate says, 'If he wasn't perfect, how can we trust the revelations?' How do you respond?
options: Prophets must be perfect or they aren't really called of God
If you discover a prophet made a mistake, you should leave the Church
Only follow prophets who never had any flaws
Prophets are imperfect humans whom God calls and works through despite their weaknesses — just like Moses, Peter, and Paul

2.tip: The Bible is full of imperfect prophets — Moses, David, Peter — and God still accomplished great things through them. (Mormon 9:31)

3.definition: Recognizing prophets as flawed humans enriches rather than diminishes faith.
3.scenarioDoneTo: While studying Church history in depth, you encounter well-documented instances of prophets expressing personal opinions that were later contradicted by revelation. A critical friend says this proves prophets have no special divine authority. How do you reason through this?
3.scenarioYouDo: While studying Church history in depth, you encounter well-documented instances of prophets expressing personal opinions that were later contradicted by revelation. A critical friend says this proves prophets have no special divine authority. How do you reason through this?

challenge.scenario: While studying Church history in depth, you encounter well-documented instances of prophets expressing personal opinions that were later contradicted by revelation. A critical friend says this proves prophets have no special divine authority. How do you reason through this?
options: Finding flaws in a prophet's character disproves their calling
Prophetic authority requires moral perfection in every aspect of life

Recognizing prophets as flawed humans enriches rather than diminishes faith — God has always chosen imperfect people to accomplish His work, and distinguishing personal opinion from revelation is essential
If a prophet's personal opinions were ever wrong, none of their revelations can be trusted

3.tip: We have this treasure in 'earthen vessels' — God's power, not prophetic perfection, is the source of divine authority. (2 Corinthians 4:7)

1.definition: God uses ordinary, imperfect people to do amazing things.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You read about how a young boy named Joseph Smith prayed in the woods and God answered him. Joseph was just a regular kid! What does this teach us?
1.scenarioYouDo: You read about how a young boy named Joseph Smith prayed in the woods and God answered him. Joseph was just a regular kid! What does this teach us?

challenge.scenario: You read about how a young boy named Joseph Smith prayed in the woods and God answered him. Joseph was just a regular kid! What does this teach us?
options: Only perfect people can do God's work
God only helps people who never make mistakes
God uses ordinary, imperfect people to do amazing things

1.tip: God doesn't wait for perfect people — He picks people with willing hearts and helps them do incredible things. (1 Samuel 16:7)

2.definition: Throughout history, God has accomplished extraordinary things through ordinary, imperfect people.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You learn that the early members of the Church faced a lot of problems and sometimes made poor decisions. A friend says this means God wasn't really in charge. What's a faithful perspective?
2.scenarioYouDo: You learn that the early members of the Church faced a lot of problems and sometimes made poor decisions. A friend says this means God wasn't really in charge. What's a faithful perspective?

challenge.scenario: You learn that the early members of the Church faced a lot of problems and sometimes made poor decisions. A friend says this means God wasn't really in charge. What's a faithful perspective?
options: Throughout history, God has accomplished extraordinary things through ordinary, imperfect people — that's a pattern, not a problem
God only uses perfect people for His work
If early Church members made mistakes, the Church can't be true
Imperfect people in church history prove God wasn't involved

2.tip: God said, 'I give unto men weakness that they may be humble.' Imperfection plus God's grace equals extraordinary results. (Ether 12:27)

3.definition: Church history reveals a consistent scriptural pattern: God works through flawed human beings.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A philosophy professor argues that the imperfections of early Church leaders and members disprove the Church's divine claims. You recognize a logical flaw in this argument based on your knowledge of scripture and history. What is it?
3.scenarioYouDo: A philosophy professor argues that the imperfections of early Church leaders and members disprove the Church's divine claims. You recognize a logical flaw in this argument based on your knowledge of scripture and history. What is it?

challenge.scenario: A philosophy professor argues that the imperfections of early Church leaders and members disprove the Church's divine claims. You recognize a logical flaw in this argument based on your knowledge of scripture and history. What is it?
options: Imperfections among early Church members invalidate the Restoration

Church history reveals a pattern consistent with all scripture: God works through flawed human beings, and their imperfections do not negate the divinity of the work — just as Peter's denial didn't negate his apostolic calling
If early Saints struggled with human weakness, the Church's foundational claims are compromised
God would never allow mistakes in the leadership of His true Church

3.tip: God calls the weak things of the world to confound the mighty. Imperfection in God's servants is the rule, not the exception. (D&C 35:13)

1.definition: People long ago lived differently, and we need to understand their world before we judge them.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You think it's strange that people in the scriptures traveled by donkey instead of car. Your dad explains that cars hadn't been invented yet. What does this teach you about judging people from the past?
1.scenarioYouDo: You think it's strange that people in the scriptures traveled by donkey instead of car. Your dad explains that cars hadn't been invented yet. What does this teach you about judging people from the past?

challenge.scenario: You think it's strange that people in the scriptures traveled by donkey instead of car. Your dad explains that cars hadn't been invented yet. What does this teach you about judging people from the past?
options: People in the past should have known everything we know today
People long ago lived differently, and we need to understand their world before we judge them
Nothing about the past matters anymore

1.tip: People in the past lived in a different world — we should try to understand their time before we judge their choices. (Ecclesiastes 3:1)

2.definition: Understanding the time period and culture helps us fairly evaluate historical events.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You read about a Church practice from the 1800s that seems really strange to you. Your seminary teacher explains what life was like in that era. Why is this context important?
2.scenarioYouDo: You read about a Church practice from the 1800s that seems really strange to you. Your seminary teacher explains what life was like in that era. Why is this context important?

challenge.scenario: You read about a Church practice from the 1800s that seems really strange to you. Your seminary teacher explains what life was like in that era. Why is this context important?
options: Judge everyone in history by today's standards
Historical context doesn't matter if something seems wrong to you
Understanding the time period and culture helps us fairly evaluate historical events and decisions
If something in church history feels uncomfortable, it must be wrong

2.tip: Judging the 1830s by 2020s standards is like blaming someone for not texting before telephones existed. Context matters. (1 Corinthians 13:12)

3.definition: Responsible historical analysis requires understanding events within their original context.
3.scenarioDoneTo: In an academic course, a critic presents 19th-century Church practices entirely through a modern lens, with no effort to explain the cultural norms of the time. You recognize this as a methodological problem. Why?
3.scenarioYouDo: In an academic course, a critic presents 19th-century Church practices entirely through a modern lens, with no effort to explain the cultural norms of the time. You recognize this as a methodological problem. Why?

challenge.scenario: In an academic course, a critic presents 19th-century Church practices entirely through a modern lens, with no effort to explain the cultural norms of the time. You recognize this as a methodological problem. Why?
options: Apply 21st-century norms retroactively to 19th-century decisions without considering context
If any historical practice seems foreign to us today, it must have been morally wrong
Dismiss historical context as excuse-making by Church apologists

Responsible historical analysis requires understanding events within their original cultural, social, and religious context before drawing conclusions — this applies to all history, not just Church history

3.tip: Good historians — secular and religious — always consider context. Decontextualized history distorts the truth, no matter who's writing it. (Alma 29:8)

1.definition: The Book of Mormon came to us in a miraculous way through God's power.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You learn that God helped Joseph Smith translate a special book from golden plates. Joseph couldn't have done it alone — he was just a farm boy! What does this show you?
1.scenarioYouDo: You learn that God helped Joseph Smith translate a special book from golden plates. Joseph couldn't have done it alone — he was just a farm boy! What does this show you?

challenge.scenario: You learn that God helped Joseph Smith translate a special book from golden plates. Joseph couldn't have done it alone — he was just a farm boy! What does this show you?
options: The Book of Mormon came to us in a miraculous way through God's power
Joseph Smith just made up the Book of Mormon by himself
The Book of Mormon isn't important

1.tip: God said, 'I am able to do mine own work.' The Book of Mormon is a miracle that shows God's power. (2 Nephi 27:23)

2.definition: The miraculous nature of the translation is evidence of God's power, regardless of the specific method used.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A friend learns that Joseph Smith used a seer stone during part of the Book of Mormon translation and says, 'That's too weird — the whole thing must be fake.' What's a thoughtful response?
2.scenarioYouDo: A friend learns that Joseph Smith used a seer stone during part of the Book of Mormon translation and says, 'That's too weird — the whole thing must be fake.' What's a thoughtful response?

challenge.scenario: A friend learns that Joseph Smith used a seer stone during part of the Book of Mormon translation and says, 'That's too weird — the whole thing must be fake.' What's a thoughtful response?
options: If the translation process seems unusual, the Book of Mormon can't be true
The miraculous nature of the translation is evidence of God's power, regardless of the specific method used
The only valid translation method would match our modern expectations exactly
Details about the translation process should shake your faith

2.tip: By small and simple things are great things brought to pass. God uses unexpected methods — what matters is the miraculous result. (Alma 37:6)

3.definition: The Book of Mormon translation was divine and miraculous, regardless of the specific physical instrument used.
3.scenarioDoneTo: While studying Church history, you learn detailed accounts of the translation process — including the use of both the Urim and Thummim and a seer stone. A critic argues these details prove the translation wasn't divine. How do you think about this carefully?
3.scenarioYouDo: While studying Church history, you learn detailed accounts of the translation process — including the use of both the Urim and Thummim and a seer stone. A critic argues these details prove the translation wasn't divine. How do you think about this carefully?

challenge.scenario: While studying Church history, you learn detailed accounts of the translation process — including the use of both the Urim and Thummim and a seer stone. A critic argues these details prove the translation wasn't divine. How do you think about this carefully?
options: The translation of the Book of Mormon — whether through the Urim and Thummim or seer stone — was a divine, miraculous process that produced profound scripture in a remarkably short time by an uneducated farm boy
If the method of translation doesn't match what we imagined, the book itself is unreliable
Unfamiliar translation details are evidence of fraud rather than divine mystery
The specific physical process of translation matters more than the content and spiritual witness of the text

3.tip: Focus on the fruit: the Book of Mormon's content, its faithfulness to Christ, and the spiritual witness it produces. The method was God's to choose. (Moroni 10:4-5)

1.definition: Some things in church history are hard to understand, but God helped His people through tough times.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You hear that some things in the early Church were really hard for the members. Your Primary teacher says God helped them through it. What can we learn from this?
1.scenarioYouDo: You hear that some things in the early Church were really hard for the members. Your Primary teacher says God helped them through it. What can we learn from this?

challenge.scenario: You hear that some things in the early Church were really hard for the members. Your Primary teacher says God helped them through it. What can we learn from this?
options: Everything about the past was exactly like today
If something in church history is hard to understand, the whole Church is wrong
Some things in church history are hard to understand, but God helped His people through difficult times

1.tip: God's ways aren't always easy to understand, but He always cares about His people, even during hard times. (Isaiah 55:8)

2.definition: Plural marriage was a difficult practice with biblical precedent that was discontinued through revelation.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A friend at school says, 'Mormons used to have multiple wives — that's so weird.' You know this is a complex topic. What's the most accurate and faithful response?
2.scenarioYouDo: A friend at school says, 'Mormons used to have multiple wives — that's so weird.' You know this is a complex topic. What's the most accurate and faithful response?

challenge.scenario: A friend at school says, 'Mormons used to have multiple wives — that's so weird.' You know this is a complex topic. What's the most accurate and faithful response?
options: Plural marriage proves the Church was never true
We should ignore anything uncomfortable in church history
Plural marriage is still practiced today in the Church
Plural marriage was a difficult practice with biblical precedent that was discontinued through prophetic revelation

2.tip: Plural marriage was real, it was hard, and it ended through revelation. Honest faith doesn't pretend difficult history didn't happen. (Jacob 2:30)

3.definition: Plural marriage was a complex chapter that must be understood in its historical and revelatory context.
3.scenarioDoneTo: Your institute class discusses plural marriage in depth. Some students are troubled by the historical details. Others want to ignore it entirely. How do you engage honestly with this difficult topic?
3.scenarioYouDo: Your institute class discusses plural marriage in depth. Some students are troubled by the historical details. Others want to ignore it entirely. How do you engage honestly with this difficult topic?

challenge.scenario: Your institute class discusses plural marriage in depth. Some students are troubled by the historical details. Others want to ignore it entirely. How do you engage honestly with this difficult topic?
options: Plural marriage is an irreconcilable problem that invalidates the entire Church
The practice should be judged entirely by modern sensibilities without any historical context

Plural marriage was a complex and painful chapter practiced within a specific historical and revelatory context, and its discontinuation through Official Declaration 1 demonstrates continuing revelation
Because plural marriage is uncomfortable, we should pretend it never happened

3.tip: Honest engagement with difficult history — not avoidance or dismissal — is the path of faithful discipleship. (D&C 132:34)

1.definition: God loves ALL of His children the same, no matter what they look like.
1.scenarioDoneTo: A friend at school says God likes some people more than others because of how they look. That makes you feel sad. What's the truth?
1.scenarioYouDo: A friend at school says God likes some people more than others because of how they look. That makes you feel sad. What's the truth?

challenge.scenario: A friend at school says God likes some people more than others because of how they look. That makes you feel sad. What's the truth?
options: God only loves some of His children
God loves ALL of His children the same, no matter what they look like
Some people are better than others because of how they look

1.tip: All are alike unto God — He loves every single one of His children equally. (2 Nephi 26:33)

2.definition: Past priesthood restrictions were wrong, and the 1978 revelation affirms God's love for all His children.
2.scenarioDoneTo: In seminary, you learn that Black members of the Church were once denied the priesthood and temple blessings. A classmate asks, 'How can the Church be true if it did that?' How do you respond?
2.scenarioYouDo: In seminary, you learn that Black members of the Church were once denied the priesthood and temple blessings. A classmate asks, 'How can the Church be true if it did that?' How do you respond?

challenge.scenario: In seminary, you learn that Black members of the Church were once denied the priesthood and temple blessings. A classmate asks, 'How can the Church be true if it did that?' How do you respond?
options: Past priesthood restrictions were wrong, and the 1978 revelation affirms that God loves all His children equally
Past policies prove God is unfair
We should never talk about uncomfortable parts of church history
If the Church changed a policy, none of its teachings can be trusted

2.tip: God is no respecter of persons. The 1978 revelation corrected a painful wrong and opened blessings to all of God's children. (Acts 10:34)

3.definition: The priesthood restriction was a serious error; the 1978 revelation corrected it, and the Church has disavowed past racial theories.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A college classmate confronts you about the Church's history with race and the priesthood, citing specific past statements by Church leaders to justify racial inequality. They question how you can remain in the Church. How do you respond with both honesty and faith?
3.scenarioYouDo: A college classmate confronts you about the Church's history with race and the priesthood, citing specific past statements by Church leaders to justify racial inequality. They question how you can remain in the Church. How do you respond with both honesty and faith?

challenge.scenario: A college classmate confronts you about the Church's history with race and the priesthood, citing specific past statements by Church leaders to justify racial inequality. They question how you can remain in the Church. How do you respond with both honesty and faith?
options: Past priesthood restrictions reflect God's true feelings about race
Because the Church once had a racial restriction, it can never be trusted on matters of equality
We should avoid discussing the priesthood restriction to protect the Church's image

The priesthood and temple restriction was a serious error that caused real pain; the 1978 revelation corrected it, and the Church has since disavowed past racial theories, affirming that all are alike unto God

3.tip: Acknowledging painful history honestly — and recognizing the revelation that corrected it — is more faithful than denial or excuse-making. (Galatians 3:28)

1.definition: God can change how we do things at church, and that's okay — He's still in charge.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your grandma tells you that church used to be different when she was young — they did some things differently. You wonder why things changed. What's the answer?
1.scenarioYouDo: Your grandma tells you that church used to be different when she was young — they did some things differently. You wonder why things changed. What's the answer?

challenge.scenario: Your grandma tells you that church used to be different when she was young — they did some things differently. You wonder why things changed. What's the answer?
options: God can change how we do things at church, and that's okay — He's still in charge
If anything at church ever changes, God must not be real
Church should always stay exactly the same forever

1.tip: We believe God will 'yet reveal many great and important things.' Changes mean God is still talking to His prophets! (Articles of Faith 1:9)

2.definition: Changes in church practices reflect ongoing revelation, not a change in eternal truth.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A friend points out that the Church has changed some policies over the years and says, 'If they keep changing things, how can it be true?' What's the important distinction?
2.scenarioYouDo: A friend points out that the Church has changed some policies over the years and says, 'If they keep changing things, how can it be true?' What's the important distinction?

challenge.scenario: A friend points out that the Church has changed some policies over the years and says, 'If they keep changing things, how can it be true?' What's the important distinction?
options: If a policy changes, the Church must not be led by God
Changes in the Church prove that prophets don't receive revelation
Changes in church practices reflect ongoing revelation, not a change in eternal truth — practices adapt, but core doctrines remain
A true church would never change any practice

2.tip: There's a difference between eternal doctrine and changeable practice. God adapts how things are done while keeping eternal truths the same. (Amos 3:7)

3.definition: Distinguishing between eternal doctrines and adaptive practices is essential to understanding Church history.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A critical article lists dozens of policy changes throughout Church history and concludes that nothing the Church teaches can be relied upon. You recognize a fundamental category error in this argument. What is it?
3.scenarioYouDo: A critical article lists dozens of policy changes throughout Church history and concludes that nothing the Church teaches can be relied upon. You recognize a fundamental category error in this argument. What is it?

challenge.scenario: A critical article lists dozens of policy changes throughout Church history and concludes that nothing the Church teaches can be relied upon. You recognize a fundamental category error in this argument. What is it?
options: Any change in church policy means previous prophets were wrong and the Church is false

Distinguishing between eternal doctrines and adaptive practices is essential — the Lord reveals adjustments in policy through prophets according to the needs of the time, while core truths remain constant
Consistent church practice across all eras is the only legitimate mark of a true church
Changes in church procedure undermine the reliability of all prophetic revelation

3.tip: Confusing policy changes with doctrinal changes is a common error. One is meant to adapt; the other is eternal. (D&C 1:38)

1.definition: The Church gives us tools and resources to help us learn about hard topics honestly.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your mom finds a question you had about church history and shows you a special page on the Church website that explains it. What does this teach you?
1.scenarioYouDo: Your mom finds a question you had about church history and shows you a special page on the Church website that explains it. What does this teach you?

challenge.scenario: Your mom finds a question you had about church history and shows you a special page on the Church website that explains it. What does this teach you?
options: Don't learn about the gospel from people you trust
Ignore hard questions and hope they go away
The Church gives us tools and resources to help us learn about hard topics honestly

1.tip: The Church wants you to learn and ask questions — they even made special articles to help you find honest answers! (D&C 88:118)

2.definition: The Church published Gospel Topics essays to honestly engage with difficult questions.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A friend says, 'The Church hides its history.' You remember that the Church published a series of Gospel Topics essays addressing difficult subjects openly. What do you tell your friend?
2.scenarioYouDo: A friend says, 'The Church hides its history.' You remember that the Church published a series of Gospel Topics essays addressing difficult subjects openly. What do you tell your friend?

challenge.scenario: A friend says, 'The Church hides its history.' You remember that the Church published a series of Gospel Topics essays addressing difficult subjects openly. What do you tell your friend?
options: The Church tries to hide difficult parts of its history
The Church published Gospel Topics essays to honestly engage with difficult questions using faithful scholarship
You should only learn about church history from critics
Hard questions should never be discussed in church settings

2.tip: The truth shall make you free. The Gospel Topics essays show the Church is willing to honestly engage with tough questions. (John 8:32)

3.definition: The Gospel Topics essays demonstrate the Church's commitment to transparency and faithful scholarship.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A professor claims the Church has never honestly addressed controversial historical topics. You know about the Gospel Topics essays on LDS.org, which address subjects like the Book of Mormon translation, plural marriage, and race and the priesthood. How do you respond?
3.scenarioYouDo: A professor claims the Church has never honestly addressed controversial historical topics. You know about the Gospel Topics essays on LDS.org, which address subjects like the Book of Mormon translation, plural marriage, and race and the priesthood. How do you respond?

challenge.scenario: A professor claims the Church has never honestly addressed controversial historical topics. You know about the Gospel Topics essays on LDS.org, which address subjects like the Book of Mormon translation, plural marriage, and race and the priesthood. How do you respond?
options: The Gospel Topics essays demonstrate the Church's commitment to transparency, providing faithful yet honest engagement with complex historical and doctrinal issues using credible scholarship
The Church has never addressed difficult historical topics publicly
Scholarly engagement with church history is fundamentally incompatible with faith
Only anti-Church sources provide honest assessments of church history

3.tip: Study to show thyself approved. The Gospel Topics essays are a powerful resource for engaging honestly with history while maintaining faith. (2 Timothy 2:15)

1.definition: God teaches us when we're ready, a little at a time.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You ask your teacher why you can't learn everything about God all at once. She says, 'Just like school, Heavenly Father teaches you when you're ready.' Why is that a good thing?
1.scenarioYouDo: You ask your teacher why you can't learn everything about God all at once. She says, 'Just like school, Heavenly Father teaches you when you're ready.' Why is that a good thing?

challenge.scenario: You ask your teacher why you can't learn everything about God all at once. She says, 'Just like school, Heavenly Father teaches you when you're ready.' Why is that a good thing?
options: God should tell us everything all at once
God teaches us when we are ready, a little at a time
If God doesn't answer right away, He must not care

1.tip: Just like you learn harder math as you get older, God teaches you deeper things when you're ready. (D&C 98:2)

2.definition: God reveals truth to prophets according to His own timing and the readiness of His children.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You wonder why God didn't reveal everything to the first prophet and just let every generation know everything from the start. Your institute teacher explains continuing revelation. What does that mean?
2.scenarioYouDo: You wonder why God didn't reveal everything to the first prophet and just let every generation know everything from the start. Your institute teacher explains continuing revelation. What does that mean?

challenge.scenario: You wonder why God didn't reveal everything to the first prophet and just let every generation know everything from the start. Your institute teacher explains continuing revelation. What does that mean?
options: God should have revealed everything to Joseph Smith on day one
If a doctrine takes time to be clarified, it was never true to begin with
Changes in understanding mean leaders are just making things up
God reveals truth to prophets according to His own timing and the readiness of His children

2.tip: God's timing isn't our timing, but His pattern of unfolding truth over time is consistent throughout scripture. (Isaiah 55:8-9)

3.definition: Continuing revelation means God unfolds truth across time as His children are prepared to receive it.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A friend argues that if the Church were truly led by God, all doctrines and policies would have been perfect from the beginning and would never change. You see this as a misunderstanding of how God has always worked. How do you explain?
3.scenarioYouDo: A friend argues that if the Church were truly led by God, all doctrines and policies would have been perfect from the beginning and would never change. You see this as a misunderstanding of how God has always worked. How do you explain?

challenge.scenario: A friend argues that if the Church were truly led by God, all doctrines and policies would have been perfect from the beginning and would never change. You see this as a misunderstanding of how God has always worked. How do you explain?
options: If God were real, all truth would have been revealed instantaneously to the first prophet
Progressive revelation proves that earlier prophets were uninspired
The principle of continuing revelation means God unfolds truth across time as His children are prepared to receive it — a pattern seen from Adam through the present dispensation
A true God would never change or add to previous instructions

3.tip: From Moses to Peter to Joseph Smith, God has always revealed truth progressively. Continuing revelation is a feature of the true Church, not a flaw. (Acts 1:7)

1.definition: You can have faith in Jesus even when things feel confusing.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You hear something at school about the Church that confuses you. You feel a little worried. But then you remember how happy you feel at church. What should you do?
1.scenarioYouDo: You hear something at school about the Church that confuses you. You feel a little worried. But then you remember how happy you feel at church. What should you do?

challenge.scenario: You hear something at school about the Church that confuses you. You feel a little worried. But then you remember how happy you feel at church. What should you do?
options: You can have faith in Jesus even when things are confusing — keep going to church and asking questions
Only people who never feel confused can believe in God
If something is hard to understand, just stop believing

1.tip: Being confused sometimes is normal — it doesn't mean your faith isn't real. Be brave and keep believing! (Joshua 1:9)

2.definition: Studying difficult topics with faith and patience can actually strengthen your testimony.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You discover a topic in church history that really bothers you. You want to either ignore it completely or leave the Church over it. Your bishop suggests a third option: study it honestly with faith. What happens when you do?
2.scenarioYouDo: You discover a topic in church history that really bothers you. You want to either ignore it completely or leave the Church over it. Your bishop suggests a third option: study it honestly with faith. What happens when you do?

challenge.scenario: You discover a topic in church history that really bothers you. You want to either ignore it completely or leave the Church over it. Your bishop suggests a third option: study it honestly with faith. What happens when you do?
options: Leave the Church the moment you encounter something complicated
Complicated topics mean the Church can't be true
Studying difficult topics with faith and patience can actually strengthen your testimony and deepen your understanding
Avoid all difficult subjects to protect your testimony

2.tip: A testimony that has wrestled with hard questions and survived is stronger than one that has never been tested. (Ether 12:6)

3.definition: Mature faith is deepened, not threatened, by honestly engaging difficult topics.
3.scenarioDoneTo: After years of faithful study, you've encountered many complex issues in Church history and doctrine. Some friends have left the Church over these issues. Others have never engaged with them at all. You've chosen a different path. What does mature faith look like?
3.scenarioYouDo: After years of faithful study, you've encountered many complex issues in Church history and doctrine. Some friends have left the Church over these issues. Others have never engaged with them at all. You've chosen a different path. What does mature faith look like?

challenge.scenario: After years of faithful study, you've encountered many complex issues in Church history and doctrine. Some friends have left the Church over these issues. Others have never engaged with them at all. You've chosen a different path. What does mature faith look like?
options: Encountering complexity in church history should lead directly to disbelief
Faithful members should never engage with difficult historical topics
Simplistic faith that avoids all complexity is stronger than faith that has been tested
Mature faith is not threatened by complexity — it is deepened by honestly engaging difficult topics with humility, study, prayer, and trust in God

3.tip: Build your foundation on the rock of Christ. When you do, the winds of complexity and difficulty cannot destroy your faith — they refine it. (Helaman 5:12)

1.definition: Talk about hard questions in a safe, kind place.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your little brother asks why some people don't believe in God. Where's the best place to talk about it?
1.scenarioYouDo: Your little brother asks why some people don't believe in God. Where's the best place to talk about it?

challenge.scenario: Your little brother asks why some people don't believe in God. Where's the best place to talk about it?
options: On the school bus where everyone can hear
At home where you can talk privately
Ignore him because it's a weird question

1.tip: Important questions deserve a quiet, safe place to talk. (Doctrine and Covenants 50:22)

2.definition: Gospel questions are best discussed in safe, respectful environments where everyone feels comfortable.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A friend at school brings up a controversial gospel topic in front of a big group. Some people start laughing. What do you do?
2.scenarioYouDo: A friend at school brings up a controversial gospel topic in front of a big group. Some people start laughing. What do you do?

challenge.scenario: A friend at school brings up a controversial gospel topic in front of a big group. Some people start laughing. What do you do?
options: Join the conversation right there so you look brave
Suggest talking about it later when it's just the two of you
Tell them they shouldn't ask questions like that
Walk away without saying anything

2.tip: Sensitive topics need a setting where people feel safe to be honest. (Proverbs 15:1)

3.definition: Creating a safe space for gospel discussions means choosing the right time, place, and tone.
3.scenarioDoneTo: During a family dinner, your uncle starts challenging the Church in front of younger cousins. The mood gets tense. What's the best approach?
3.scenarioYouDo: During a family dinner, your uncle starts challenging the Church in front of younger cousins. The mood gets tense. What's the best approach?

challenge.scenario: During a family dinner, your uncle starts challenging the Church in front of younger cousins. The mood gets tense. What's the best approach?
options: Argue back to defend the Church right then
Tell your uncle he's being inappropriate
Quietly suggest continuing the conversation later in a more private setting
Leave the table and refuse to engage

3.tip: The right setting can turn a potential argument into a meaningful conversation. (Doctrine and Covenants 50:22)

1.definition: Listen carefully when someone shares how they feel.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your friend says she's confused about something she heard at church. What should you do first?
1.scenarioYouDo: Your friend says she's confused about something she heard at church. What should you do first?

challenge.scenario: Your friend says she's confused about something she heard at church. What should you do first?
options: Listen to what she's confused about
Tell her she shouldn't feel confused
Change the subject to something fun

1.tip: Listening shows someone you care about their feelings. (James 1:19)

2.definition: Listen to understand the person, not just to plan your response.
2.scenarioDoneTo: Your friend is telling you about something in Church history that bothers him. You already know the answer. What do you do?
2.scenarioYouDo: Your friend is telling you about something in Church history that bothers him. You already know the answer. What do you do?

challenge.scenario: Your friend is telling you about something in Church history that bothers him. You already know the answer. What do you do?
options: Interrupt and share what you know before he finishes
Nod but secretly think of your rebuttal
Let him finish, then ask what bothers him most
Tell him he's overthinking it

2.tip: People need to feel heard before they're ready to hear your perspective. (James 1:19)

3.definition: Truly listening means seeking to understand someone's heart, not just their words.
3.scenarioDoneTo: Your roommate says she doesn't feel God's love anymore and is questioning everything. She's emotional and struggling. What do you do?
3.scenarioYouDo: Your roommate says she doesn't feel God's love anymore and is questioning everything. She's emotional and struggling. What do you do?

challenge.scenario: Your roommate says she doesn't feel God's love anymore and is questioning everything. She's emotional and struggling. What do you do?
options: Share a talk by a General Authority that addresses her concern
Tell her to read her scriptures more and pray harder
Ask her to share more about what she's feeling and listen without interrupting
Remind her that everyone goes through hard times

3.tip: Sometimes the most powerful thing you can do is listen with your whole heart. (Mosiah 18:9)

1.definition: Never make fun of someone's question.
1.scenarioDoneTo: In Primary, a kid asks a question that sounds silly to you. What should you do?
1.scenarioYouDo: In Primary, a kid asks a question that sounds silly to you. What should you do?

challenge.scenario: In Primary, a kid asks a question that sounds silly to you. What should you do?
options: Laugh and say 'That's a dumb question'
Roll your eyes at your friend
Listen respectfully because all questions matter

1.tip: Every question matters, even if it sounds silly at first. (Ephesians 4:29)

2.definition: Mocking someone's sincere question can damage their trust and willingness to keep seeking.
2.scenarioDoneTo: In seminary, a classmate asks whether dinosaurs are mentioned in the scriptures. A few people snicker. How do you respond?
2.scenarioYouDo: In seminary, a classmate asks whether dinosaurs are mentioned in the scriptures. A few people snicker. How do you respond?

challenge.scenario: In seminary, a classmate asks whether dinosaurs are mentioned in the scriptures. A few people snicker. How do you respond?
options: Laugh along — it was kind of a funny question
Say that's a great question and you've wondered about that too
Ignore the question and move on
Tell the person to Google it later

2.tip: If someone is brave enough to ask, be kind enough to respect it. (Ephesians 4:2)

3.definition: Dismissing or mocking a person's doubts can cause lasting spiritual harm and push them further from the gospel.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A friend shares on social media that he's struggling with his testimony. In a group chat, someone screenshots it and starts making jokes. What do you do?
3.scenarioYouDo: A friend shares on social media that he's struggling with his testimony. In a group chat, someone screenshots it and starts making jokes. What do you do?

challenge.scenario: A friend shares on social media that he's struggling with his testimony. In a group chat, someone screenshots it and starts making jokes. What do you do?
options: Ignore it — it's not your business
Join in lightly so you don't look too serious
Speak up that it's not okay to mock someone's struggles and reach out to your friend privately
React with a laughing emoji but don't say anything

3.tip: How you treat someone's doubts can either open or close the door to their faith. (3 Nephi 12:11)

1.definition: Share what you know kindly, not like a know-it-all.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You know the answer to a gospel question your friend asked. How should you share it?
1.scenarioYouDo: You know the answer to a gospel question your friend asked. How should you share it?

challenge.scenario: You know the answer to a gospel question your friend asked. How should you share it?
options: Say 'Duh, everyone knows that!'
Brag about how much you know
Share gently and say 'I learned something cool about that'

1.tip: People listen better when you share with kindness, not pride. (Doctrine and Covenants 121:41)

2.definition: Sharing what you know with humility invites the Spirit; being arrogant pushes it away.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You've studied a lot about a gospel topic your friend is questioning. You feel confident you have the right answer. What's the best approach?
2.scenarioYouDo: You've studied a lot about a gospel topic your friend is questioning. You feel confident you have the right answer. What's the best approach?

challenge.scenario: You've studied a lot about a gospel topic your friend is questioning. You feel confident you have the right answer. What's the best approach?
options: Tell them you've studied this a lot and explain exactly what they need to understand
Say 'Here's what I found helpful — you might see it differently, and that's okay'
Keep quiet so you don't seem like a show-off
Text them links to articles so they can figure it out on their own

2.tip: Humility doesn't mean you stay silent — it means you share without putting yourself above others. (Doctrine and Covenants 121:41-42)

3.definition: Sharing truth with humility means recognizing you don't have all the answers, even when you have some.
3.scenarioDoneTo: In a religion class discussion, you feel strongly about a doctrinal point and someone disagrees. You're sure you're right. How should you handle it?
3.scenarioYouDo: In a religion class discussion, you feel strongly about a doctrinal point and someone disagrees. You're sure you're right. How should you handle it?

challenge.scenario: In a religion class discussion, you feel strongly about a doctrinal point and someone disagrees. You're sure you're right. How should you handle it?
options: Stay quiet to avoid conflict
Tell the teacher to settle the debate
Assert your view firmly and cite all your sources to prove your point
Acknowledge their perspective and share yours as your understanding, not the final word

3.tip: The strongest testimony is shared with gentleness, not force. (Doctrine and Covenants 121:41-42)

1.definition: It's okay to say 'I don't know — let's find out!'
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your younger sister asks you a gospel question you don't know the answer to. What do you say?
1.scenarioYouDo: Your younger sister asks you a gospel question you don't know the answer to. What do you say?

challenge.scenario: Your younger sister asks you a gospel question you don't know the answer to. What do you say?
options: Make up something that sounds right
'I'm not sure, but let's look it up together!'
'Ask Mom, I don't know anything'

1.tip: Not knowing is fine — what matters is being willing to learn. (Doctrine and Covenants 88:118)

2.definition: Admitting you don't have all the answers shows honesty, not weakness.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A friend in seminary asks you about a tough gospel topic. You've never thought about it before. What do you do?
2.scenarioYouDo: A friend in seminary asks you about a tough gospel topic. You've never thought about it before. What do you do?

challenge.scenario: A friend in seminary asks you about a tough gospel topic. You've never thought about it before. What do you do?
options: Pretend you know and give a vague answer
Tell them the answer isn't important
Change the subject so you don't look unprepared
Say 'I'm not sure, but that's a great question — let's study it together'

2.tip: Honesty about what you don't know builds more trust than pretending you have all the answers. (Doctrine and Covenants 88:118)

3.definition: Saying 'I don't know, but let's find out together' is one of the most faith-building things you can say.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A questioning friend asks you a deeply complex doctrinal question that even scholars debate. You don't have a clear answer. How do you respond?
3.scenarioYouDo: A questioning friend asks you a deeply complex doctrinal question that even scholars debate. You don't have a clear answer. How do you respond?

challenge.scenario: A questioning friend asks you a deeply complex doctrinal question that even scholars debate. You don't have a clear answer. How do you respond?
options: Give your best guess so they have something to hold onto
Admit you're not sure and offer to study it together over the coming weeks
Tell them some questions aren't meant to be answered in this life
Redirect them to talk to the bishop instead

3.tip: Searching together can strengthen both of your testimonies more than any quick answer. (Doctrine and Covenants 88:118)

1.definition: Say 'I feel' instead of 'You should.'
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your friend says church is boring. How do you respond?
1.scenarioYouDo: Your friend says church is boring. How do you respond?

challenge.scenario: Your friend says church is boring. How do you respond?
options: 'You should pay better attention!'
'I feel happy at church because I learn about Jesus'
'That's a bad thing to say'

1.tip: Sharing how you feel is kinder than telling others what to do. (Proverbs 15:1)

2.definition: Using 'I' statements keeps conversations safe and reduces defensiveness.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A friend says they think some Church rules are unfair. You disagree. What's the best way to share your view?
2.scenarioYouDo: A friend says they think some Church rules are unfair. You disagree. What's the best way to share your view?

challenge.scenario: A friend says they think some Church rules are unfair. You disagree. What's the best way to share your view?
options: 'You're wrong — those rules are there for a reason'
'You just don't understand yet'
'I see it differently — here's what helps me understand those guidelines'
'You should pray about it more'

2.tip: 'I' statements share your heart without attacking theirs. (Proverbs 15:1)

3.definition: Speaking from personal experience rather than issuing commands invites dialogue instead of defensiveness.
3.scenarioDoneTo: In a small group discussion, someone says the Church's stance on a social issue is wrong. Several people agree. You see it differently. How do you contribute?
3.scenarioYouDo: In a small group discussion, someone says the Church's stance on a social issue is wrong. Several people agree. You see it differently. How do you contribute?

challenge.scenario: In a small group discussion, someone says the Church's stance on a social issue is wrong. Several people agree. You see it differently. How do you contribute?
options: Say 'You're all missing the point — here's what the prophet said'
Stay silent because you're outnumbered
Share your personal experience: 'I've wrestled with this too, and here's how I've come to see it'
Challenge them to read specific talks that prove your point

3.tip: Personal experience is harder to argue with than abstract commands. (Doctrine and Covenants 121:41)

1.definition: The Holy Ghost leaves when people argue.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You and your brother start arguing about a scripture story. Voices get louder. What should you do?
1.scenarioYouDo: You and your brother start arguing about a scripture story. Voices get louder. What should you do?

challenge.scenario: You and your brother start arguing about a scripture story. Voices get louder. What should you do?
options: Keep arguing until you win
Take a deep breath and calmly say 'Let's ask Dad together'
Yell louder so he'll listen

1.tip: When voices go up, the Spirit goes out. Keep it calm. (3 Nephi 11:29)

2.definition: Contention drives away the Spirit and prevents anyone from learning or growing.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A gospel discussion in your youth group starts getting heated. Two people are raising their voices. What's the right response?
2.scenarioYouDo: A gospel discussion in your youth group starts getting heated. Two people are raising their voices. What's the right response?

challenge.scenario: A gospel discussion in your youth group starts getting heated. Two people are raising their voices. What's the right response?
options: Pick a side and help them debate
Jump in with a louder opinion to settle it
Gently suggest taking a pause and remembering everyone is on the same team
Walk away without a word

2.tip: You can discuss passionately without becoming contentious. (3 Nephi 11:29)

3.definition: The Savior taught that contention is not of Him — the Spirit cannot teach when people argue.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You're in a group text where a gospel topic has turned into a heated argument. People are getting personal. What do you do?
3.scenarioYouDo: You're in a group text where a gospel topic has turned into a heated argument. People are getting personal. What do you do?

challenge.scenario: You're in a group text where a gospel topic has turned into a heated argument. People are getting personal. What do you do?
options: Send a well-crafted message defending the truth
Post a scripture to prove the other side wrong
Call out that the conversation has become contentious and suggest continuing in person over a calm conversation
Mute the chat and ignore it

3.tip: Winning an argument but losing the Spirit is never a real victory. (3 Nephi 11:29-30)

1.definition: Focus on what you and others agree on.
1.scenarioDoneTo: You and your friend believe different things about heaven. What should you focus on?
1.scenarioYouDo: You and your friend believe different things about heaven. What should you focus on?

challenge.scenario: You and your friend believe different things about heaven. What should you focus on?
options: Who's right and who's wrong
That you both love God and want to do good
Why their church is different from yours

1.tip: Focus on what brings you together, not what pulls you apart. (Mosiah 18:21)

2.definition: Focusing on shared beliefs and values strengthens relationships even when you disagree.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You and a member friend disagree about a church policy. The conversation is getting awkward. What's the best approach?
2.scenarioYouDo: You and a member friend disagree about a church policy. The conversation is getting awkward. What's the best approach?

challenge.scenario: You and a member friend disagree about a church policy. The conversation is getting awkward. What's the best approach?
options: Keep debating until one of you changes your mind
Avoid the friend so you don't have to discuss it again
Post about it online to get more opinions
Acknowledge the disagreement and focus on what you both believe

2.tip: Unity doesn't mean you agree on everything — it means you value each other more than the disagreement. (Mosiah 18:21)

3.definition: Focusing on common ground builds bridges that can carry the weight of honest disagreement.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You're discussing faith with a coworker from another religion. You disagree on several doctrines, but you share many values. How do you approach the conversation?
3.scenarioYouDo: You're discussing faith with a coworker from another religion. You disagree on several doctrines, but you share many values. How do you approach the conversation?

challenge.scenario: You're discussing faith with a coworker from another religion. You disagree on several doctrines, but you share many values. How do you approach the conversation?
options: Focus on explaining why your beliefs are more correct
Find and emphasize your shared values like family, service, and kindness while respecting differences
Avoid discussing religion entirely to prevent conflict
Agree with everything they say to keep peace

3.tip: Shared values are the foundation for deeper, more honest conversations. (Articles of Faith 1:13)

1.definition: You can disagree and still be friends.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your friend says she doesn't believe in the Book of Mormon. You do. What should you do?
