Critical thinking is a gift meant to seek truth — using it only to deconstruct without seeking to understand is misusing a valuable tool

2.tip: A mind that only tears down eventually has nothing left to stand on — true critical thinking also builds. (Moroni 7:13-14)

3.definition: Critical thinking is a divine gift that becomes destructive when directed solely at deconstruction — its highest purpose is the rigorous, honest pursuit of truth across all domains, including spiritual truth.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A fellow student who prides themselves on being a 'free thinker' says that being truly rational means deconstructing all religious belief. You notice they never apply the same scrutiny to their own assumptions.
3.scenarioYouDo: A fellow student who prides themselves on being a 'free thinker' says that being truly rational means deconstructing all religious belief. You notice they never apply the same scrutiny to their own assumptions.

challenge.scenario: A fellow student who prides themselves on being a 'free thinker' says that being truly rational means deconstructing all religious belief. You notice they never apply the same scrutiny to their own assumptions.
options: Agree — truly rational people reject all religion
Avoid critical thinking entirely to protect your faith

Acknowledge that genuine critical thinking requires examining ALL assumptions — including the assumption that rationality precludes faith — and that the highest use of critical thinking is pursuing truth wherever it leads, including spiritual truth
Tell them critical thinking is irrelevant to real life

3.tip: Anyone can deconstruct — the real challenge is having the intellectual courage to pursue truth wherever it leads, even into faith. (D&C 88:40)

1.definition: When you don't know the answer, it's smart to be humble — not to make fun of what others believe.
1.scenarioDoneTo: A kid at school laughs at you for believing in God and says, 'Nobody smart believes that stuff.' It hurts your feelings.
1.scenarioYouDo: A kid at school laughs at you for believing in God and says, 'Nobody smart believes that stuff.' It hurts your feelings.

challenge.scenario: A kid at school laughs at you for believing in God and says, 'Nobody smart believes that stuff.' It hurts your feelings.
options: Maybe they're right and you should stop believing
Everyone who believes in God is wrong
Being humble enough to believe in things you can't see takes real courage — and lots of smart people believe in God too!

1.tip: The bravest thing is to stay humble and keep believing, even when others don't understand. (Proverbs 3:5-6)

2.definition: The smartest response to uncertainty is humility, not cynicism — cynicism pretends to have answers it doesn't actually have.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A classmate acts like they've figured out that religion is for 'simple' people and says that being skeptical about everything is the sign of a mature mind.
2.scenarioYouDo: A classmate acts like they've figured out that religion is for 'simple' people and says that being skeptical about everything is the sign of a mature mind.

challenge.scenario: A classmate acts like they've figured out that religion is for 'simple' people and says that being skeptical about everything is the sign of a mature mind.
options: Humility in the face of uncertainty is far wiser than cynicism — skepticism without humility is just arrogance dressed up as intelligence
They're right — being skeptical means you're smarter
If you can't prove something, you should mock it
Doubting everything is always the wisest approach

2.tip: Cynicism is easy — humility takes real intellectual strength. (2 Nephi 9:28)

3.definition: In the face of genuine uncertainty, intellectual humility is a far more rational and productive stance than cynicism, which masquerades as wisdom while merely substituting one set of unexamined assumptions for another.
3.scenarioDoneTo: An intelligent friend has become deeply cynical about faith and religion, claiming that sophisticated thinkers naturally outgrow belief. But you notice their cynicism hasn't given them answers — just a sense of superiority.
3.scenarioYouDo: An intelligent friend has become deeply cynical about faith and religion, claiming that sophisticated thinkers naturally outgrow belief. But you notice their cynicism hasn't given them answers — just a sense of superiority.

challenge.scenario: An intelligent friend has become deeply cynical about faith and religion, claiming that sophisticated thinkers naturally outgrow belief. But you notice their cynicism hasn't given them answers — just a sense of superiority.
options: Admire their sophistication — cynicism is the natural endpoint of deep thinking
Agree that outgrowing faith is a sign of maturity
Avoid the conversation entirely because you can't compete intellectually

Point out that cynicism isn't the same as wisdom — intellectual humility acknowledges what we don't know and remains open to all avenues of truth, while cynicism simply closes doors and calls it enlightenment

3.tip: The cynic thinks they've arrived at the top — the humble person realizes the mountain is taller than they thought. (2 Nephi 9:28-29)

1.definition: Many super-smart people — scientists, doctors, and teachers — also believe in God and have strong faith.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Someone at school says, 'Scientists don't believe in God.' But you know that's not true.
1.scenarioYouDo: Someone at school says, 'Scientists don't believe in God.' But you know that's not true.

challenge.scenario: Someone at school says, 'Scientists don't believe in God.' But you know that's not true.
options: That's true — no smart people believe in God
Lots of amazing scientists and thinkers have been people of deep faith — being smart and believing in God go together!
If a scientist believes in God, they must not be a very good scientist

1.tip: Some of the smartest people who ever lived were also some of the most faithful! (Abraham 3:19)

2.definition: Many brilliant scholars throughout history and today are also people of deep faith — intelligence and belief are not mutually exclusive.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A friend says they want to pursue a career in science but worries that being a scientist and being a faithful church member are incompatible.
2.scenarioYouDo: A friend says they want to pursue a career in science but worries that being a scientist and being a faithful church member are incompatible.

challenge.scenario: A friend says they want to pursue a career in science but worries that being a scientist and being a faithful church member are incompatible.
options: They're right — science and faith can't coexist in one person
Faith and intellectual achievement go hand-in-hand for many of the world's top scholars and scientists — their career and their faith can strengthen each other
They should choose — either be a scientist or be faithful, not both
Scientific careers always lead to loss of faith

2.tip: The lab coat and the Sunday suit aren't mutually exclusive — some people wear both beautifully. (D&C 93:36)

3.definition: The claim that intellectual rigor and religious faith are incompatible is empirically falsified by the countless accomplished scholars, scientists, and thinkers — past and present — who maintain deep religious conviction alongside professional excellence.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A graduate school colleague makes a sweeping generalization that 'serious academics don't do religion' — unaware that many of your most respected professors are privately people of faith.
3.scenarioYouDo: A graduate school colleague makes a sweeping generalization that 'serious academics don't do religion' — unaware that many of your most respected professors are privately people of faith.

challenge.scenario: A graduate school colleague makes a sweeping generalization that 'serious academics don't do religion' — unaware that many of your most respected professors are privately people of faith.
options: Help them see that the stereotype of the faithless intellectual is empirically false — many of today's and history's most accomplished scholars hold deep religious convictions, and their faith informs, rather than undermines, their intellectual work
Agree — serious scholars don't have time for religion
Religion is irrelevant to academic success
Keep your faith completely hidden in academic settings

3.tip: The world's greatest thinkers aren't all on one side — genius shows up in the lab and in the chapel alike. (D&C 88:79)

1.definition: You don't have to choose between being smart and having faith — you can be both!
1.scenarioDoneTo: A friend says, 'Are you going to be a thinker or a believer when you grow up?' — like you have to pick one.
1.scenarioYouDo: A friend says, 'Are you going to be a thinker or a believer when you grow up?' — like you have to pick one.

challenge.scenario: A friend says, 'Are you going to be a thinker or a believer when you grow up?' — like you have to pick one.
options: You do have to pick — you can't be both
You can absolutely be a thinker AND a believer — God wants you to be both!
Believing in God means you shouldn't think too hard about things

1.tip: Being smart and having faith isn't 'having it both ways' — it's having it the right way! (D&C 88:118)

2.definition: Setting up a false choice between being intelligent and being faithful is a trap — God never asks you to sacrifice one for the other.
2.scenarioDoneTo: You overhear someone say, 'The smarter kids at school are the ones who don't believe anymore.' That stings because you're both a good student and a person of faith.
2.scenarioYouDo: You overhear someone say, 'The smarter kids at school are the ones who don't believe anymore.' That stings because you're both a good student and a person of faith.

challenge.scenario: You overhear someone say, 'The smarter kids at school are the ones who don't believe anymore.' That stings because you're both a good student and a person of faith.
options: Intelligence naturally leads people away from faith
They must be right — maybe you should hide your faith to seem smarter

The idea that you must choose between intelligence and faith is a false dichotomy — many of the deepest thinkers are people of strong faith, and your intelligence can actually strengthen your testimony
Being faithful means you'll struggle academically

2.tip: The false choice between being smart and being faithful is one of the adversary's oldest tricks — don't buy it. (2 Nephi 9:29)

3.definition: The dichotomy between intellectual capability and religious faith is a false construct — God commands the cultivation of both, and the most integrated disciples are those who refuse to truncate either their intellectual or spiritual capacities.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You're applying for a prestigious academic program and a mentor subtly suggests that your religious involvement might undermine your credibility as a serious thinker. You know your faith and your intellect are both integral to who you are.
3.scenarioYouDo: You're applying for a prestigious academic program and a mentor subtly suggests that your religious involvement might undermine your credibility as a serious thinker. You know your faith and your intellect are both integral to who you are.

challenge.scenario: You're applying for a prestigious academic program and a mentor subtly suggests that your religious involvement might undermine your credibility as a serious thinker. You know your faith and your intellect are both integral to who you are.
options: Hide your faith to be taken seriously in academic circles

Recognize that genuine intellectual integrity means refusing to artificially separate your identity — your faith and your intellect both inform your scholarship, and authentic integration is more honest than forced compartmentalization
Accept that faithful people will always face intellectual limitations
Religion has no place in serious academic work

3.tip: A mind that is both rigorous and reverent is the most powerful combination there is — don't let anyone convince you to amputate half of who you are. (D&C 130:18-19)

1.definition: God expects you to use your mind to think AND your heart to feel — He gave you both for a reason!
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your teacher says that learning about God is like using two hands — your mind to think and your heart to feel. Both work together!
1.scenarioYouDo: Your teacher says that learning about God is like using two hands — your mind to think and your heart to feel. Both work together!

challenge.scenario: Your teacher says that learning about God is like using two hands — your mind to think and your heart to feel. Both work together!
options: Only your heart matters — stop thinking
Only your brain matters — stop feeling
God wants you to use your mind AND your heart together to understand truth!

1.tip: Think of your mind and your heart as best friends — they work better together! (D&C 8:2)

2.definition: God expects us to use both our minds and our hearts in seeking truth — intellectual inquiry and spiritual sensitivity are both essential tools.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A seminary teacher says, 'Just feel the Spirit — don't overthink it,' while your school teacher says, 'Just use logic — feelings are unreliable.' You think both are partly right and partly wrong.
2.scenarioYouDo: A seminary teacher says, 'Just feel the Spirit — don't overthink it,' while your school teacher says, 'Just use logic — feelings are unreliable.' You think both are partly right and partly wrong.

challenge.scenario: A seminary teacher says, 'Just feel the Spirit — don't overthink it,' while your school teacher says, 'Just use logic — feelings are unreliable.' You think both are partly right and partly wrong.
options: Only follow your heart — logic is dangerous
Only follow logic — feelings are always unreliable
Just pick whichever one is more convenient at the moment
God gave you both a mind and a heart because seeking truth requires both — intellectual inquiry and spiritual sensitivity work together to give a more complete picture

2.tip: A mind without heart is cold; a heart without mind is blind — God asks you to bring both to the table. (D&C 8:2-3)

3.definition: The divine expectation to engage both mind and heart reflects the full spectrum of human capacity for apprehending truth — reducing religion to mere emotion or mere intellect distorts God's intent for how we come to know.
3.scenarioDoneTo: You notice that some members in your ward operate purely on emotion ('I just feel it!') while some operate purely on intellect ('I need everything to make logical sense'). You believe God intends something more holistic.
3.scenarioYouDo: You notice that some members in your ward operate purely on emotion ('I just feel it!') while some operate purely on intellect ('I need everything to make logical sense'). You believe God intends something more holistic.

challenge.scenario: You notice that some members in your ward operate purely on emotion ('I just feel it!') while some operate purely on intellect ('I need everything to make logical sense'). You believe God intends something more holistic.
options: The emotional approach is always correct — feeling trumps thinking
The intellectual approach is always correct — logic trumps feeling

God's model of truth-seeking integrates both cognitive rigor and spiritual receptivity — those who reduce faith to either emotion alone or intellect alone are operating with an incomplete picture of how revelation works
Neither approach works — faith is ultimately unknowable

3.tip: God speaks to your mind AND your heart — a faith built on both is a faith that can weather any storm. (D&C 9:7-8)

1.definition: Prophets are called by God to lead His church, but they are still regular people who can make mistakes — just like everyone else.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your teacher tells a story about a prophet who said something kind of grumpy once, and another kid says, 'I thought prophets were perfect!'
1.scenarioYouDo: You learn that a prophet once made a mistake, and you wonder if that means he wasn't really a prophet.

challenge.scenario: You find out a prophet once said something that was wrong. What should you think?
options: Prophets are still human and can make mistakes — that doesn't mean God didn't call them
If a prophet ever made a mistake, nothing they said can be trusted
Prophets are perfect and never make mistakes

1.tip: God uses imperfect people to do His work — and that includes prophets.

2.definition: Prophets are divinely called but not infallible. They can have personal opinions that aren't official doctrine, and recognizing this is actually a sign of mature faith.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A friend reads an old quote from a past prophet that sounds really strange and says, 'See? The whole church is wrong.'
2.scenarioYouDo: You discover that a past prophet expressed a personal opinion that the church no longer teaches, and you're not sure what to do with that.

challenge.scenario: You find an old quote from a prophet that contradicts current church teaching. What's the best response?
options: Conclude the church must be false
Recognize that prophets can have personal opinions that aren't binding doctrine, and focus on what's consistently taught
Ignore it and pretend you never saw it
Decide that only modern prophets matter and everything before them is worthless

2.tip: Personal opinions and official doctrine aren't the same thing — even from a prophet.

3.definition: Prophetic calling doesn't erase humanity. Prophets speak for God on matters of doctrine and church direction, but they also carry personal views, cultural context, and human limitations — distinguishing between the two is part of faithful discipleship.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A college classmate presents a list of controversial statements by past church leaders and argues they disqualify the church's truth claims entirely.
3.scenarioYouDo: You're studying church history and encounter statements from prophets that reflect the racial or cultural biases of their time, and you're unsettled.

challenge.scenario: You encounter a historically problematic statement from a past prophet. How do you process it faithfully and honestly?
options: Defend everything any prophet ever said as God's will
Abandon your faith entirely because prophets weren't perfect
Acknowledge human limitation while recognizing that prophetic authority operates within a pattern of sustained, consistent teaching confirmed by the Spirit
Conclude that prophets are no different from anyone else and their counsel doesn't matter

3.tip: The standard of truth isn't one isolated quote — it's the weight of consistent, confirmed teaching across time.

1.definition: When the prophet gives counsel, you should follow it — and you can also pray to Heavenly Father to help you know it's right.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your dad tells you to wear a coat because it's cold. You trust him, but you also look outside to see for yourself.
1.scenarioYouDo: The prophet asks families to do something new, and your parents explain why — but you also want to feel it's right in your heart.

challenge.scenario: The prophet asks everyone to do something and you're not sure why. What should you do?
options: Ignore the prophet because you don't understand
Follow the counsel and ask Heavenly Father to help you understand
Wait until you fully understand before doing anything

1.tip: Following the prophet and asking God for your own confirmation — that's how faith works together.

2.definition: Prophetic counsel deserves your trust and obedience, but God also wants you to seek personal spiritual confirmation — following and confirming aren't opposites.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A friend says, 'I just do whatever the prophet says without thinking,' and another friend says, 'I don't follow anyone blindly.' You realize there might be a middle ground.
2.scenarioYouDo: The prophet gives counsel that feels hard or confusing, and you want to be obedient but also want to know for yourself.

challenge.scenario: The prophet gives counsel you don't fully understand yet. What's the best approach?
options: Follow blindly and never ask questions
Refuse to follow until you get a personal revelation
Follow with faith while seeking your own confirmation through prayer and study
Tell everyone the prophet is wrong because you haven't felt it yet

2.tip: Obedience and personal revelation aren't competing — they're partners.

3.definition: Mature discipleship involves a dual commitment: sustaining prophetic counsel with faith while actively seeking personal spiritual witness. This isn't doubt — it's the pattern God established.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A roommate accuses you of 'blind obedience' for following the prophet, while another says seeking personal confirmation shows 'weak faith.' Both miss the balance.
3.scenarioYouDo: You're wrestling with a prophetic policy that feels counterintuitive, and you want to be faithful without checking your own conscience at the door.

challenge.scenario: A friend says real faith means never questioning prophetic counsel. How do you respond?
options: Agree — questioning is always a sign of weak faith
Explain that seeking personal confirmation through the Spirit is the pattern God established — it strengthens faith rather than undermining it
Say the prophet is just another opinion to consider
Argue that personal feelings always override prophetic counsel

3.tip: Oliver Cowdery was told to 'study it out in your mind' before asking God (D&C 9:8). Thinking and faith work together.

1.definition: God promised that the prophet leading the whole church will never take everyone in the wrong direction — but that doesn't mean every single leader is perfect.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your bishop says something in a meeting that doesn't sound quite right, and your mom says, 'Bishops are good men, but they're learning too.'
1.scenarioYouDo: You hear the prophet speak in General Conference and feel peaceful, but then a leader at church says something that doesn't feel the same way.

challenge.scenario: A leader at church says something that doesn't feel right to you. What should you do?
options: Assume every church leader is always right about everything
Remember that local leaders can make mistakes even though the prophet won't lead the whole church astray
Decide the whole church is wrong

1.tip: God protects the big direction of the church, but every leader is still learning — just like you.

2.definition: The Lord promised He won't let the President of the Church lead the members astray as a whole — but individual leaders at any level can still make errors in judgment or express personal opinions.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A stake leader makes a policy decision that feels harsh and unfair, and some members wonder if this means the church itself is off track.
2.scenarioYouDo: A church leader gives you counsel that feels off, and you're confused about what it means to 'follow the leaders.'

challenge.scenario: A local leader gives you advice that feels wrong. What's the wisest approach?
options: Obey without question because all leaders speak for God
Pray about it — individual leaders can make mistakes, even if the overall direction of the church is guided by God
Leave the church because one leader was wrong
Post about it on social media to call them out

2.tip: The promise that the church won't be led astray applies to the prophetic direction, not to every opinion of every leader.

3.definition: The promise that the President of the Church will not lead members astray is a macro-level assurance about God's oversight of His church's direction — it doesn't guarantee that every local decision, policy application, or leader's counsel is free from human error.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A friend who left the church points to a painful experience with a bishop as evidence the whole institution is broken.
3.scenarioYouDo: You've experienced leadership roulette — your experience in the church has depended heavily on which particular leaders you had — and you're wrestling with what the 'never lead astray' promise really means.

challenge.scenario: Someone argues that if any church leader makes a mistake, the 'never lead astray' promise is false. How do you respond?
options: Agree — any error disproves the church
Refuse to acknowledge that any leader has ever erred
Explain that the promise applies to the prophetic direction of the church as a whole, not to the perfection of individual leaders — and that the distinction matters
Say the promise only applies to things you personally agree with

3.tip: Trusting God's hand in the church doesn't require believing every human agent is flawless.

1.definition: Sustaining means you support your leaders, pray for them, and try to help them — not that you think they're perfect.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your class raises their hands to sustain the new Primary president, and your teacher explains it means you'll help her and pray for her.
1.scenarioYouDo: You raise your hand to sustain someone at church and wonder what that actually means.

challenge.scenario: What does it mean when you raise your hand to sustain a leader?
options: It means you'll support them, pray for them, and help them do their job
It means you think they're perfect
It doesn't mean anything — it's just tradition

1.tip: Sustaining is like being a teammate — you're saying, 'I'll help you succeed.'

2.definition: Sustaining leaders means offering your support, prayers, and willingness to serve — while also being honest if something is genuinely wrong.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A youth leader makes a decision you disagree with, and some kids start talking behind their back instead of offering feedback respectfully.
2.scenarioYouDo: You disagree with how your Young Men/Young Women leader handles something, and you're not sure if sustaining means you have to keep quiet.

challenge.scenario: You disagree with a decision your church leader made. What does sustaining actually look like?
options: Never disagree with anything ever
Support them and pray for them, while also being able to respectfully share concerns when appropriate
Talk behind their back to other members
Refuse to participate until they change their mind

2.tip: Sustaining isn't silence — it's loyal, prayerful support that can include honest conversation.

3.definition: Sustaining is an active covenant to support, pray for, and work alongside called leaders — while recognizing that genuine concerns can be raised through proper channels without undermining the work.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A ward member sustains every leader publicly but privately criticizes them constantly, creating a toxic social dynamic.
3.scenarioYouDo: You have legitimate concerns about how a leader is handling their calling, and you want to be sustaining without being passive or dishonest.

challenge.scenario: You know a leader is making a significant error that's hurting people. What does faithful sustaining look like?
options: Stay silent because sustaining means never raising concerns
Go public on social media to force change
Raise your concern privately with appropriate leaders while continuing to support the broader work — sustaining includes honest stewardship
Decide sustaining is meaningless and stop participating

3.tip: Sustaining is about building up, not covering up. Honest concern through the right channel is sustaining at its best.

1.definition: There's a difference between what the prophet tells everyone and what one leader says to you — both matter, but in different ways.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your Primary teacher tells you something different from what the prophet said in conference, and you're confused about which one to follow.
1.scenarioYouDo: You hear the prophet say one thing and your Sunday School teacher say something a little different, and you're not sure who's right.

challenge.scenario: Your teacher at church says something different from what the prophet taught. What do you do?
options: Get angry at your teacher
Politely remember that the prophet speaks for the whole church — your teacher might have a different opinion
Decide the prophet must be wrong

1.tip: The prophet's words in General Conference are for everyone. A teacher's opinion might just be for that lesson.

2.definition: General counsel from prophets and apostles carries more weight than local leader opinions. Learning to distinguish between the two helps you navigate faith with clarity.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A seminary teacher shares a strong personal opinion as if it's doctrine, and several students now believe it's official church teaching.
2.scenarioYouDo: A local leader gives you counsel that seems to contradict something you've heard in General Conference, and you need to figure out which carries more weight.

challenge.scenario: A local leader tells you something that contradicts recent General Conference teaching. How do you navigate this?
options: Always assume local leaders outrank General Conference
Recognize that General Conference counsel from the First Presidency and Quorum of the Twelve carries greater authority than individual local opinions
Ignore both and make up your own doctrine
Assume there's a conspiracy between leaders

2.tip: Official doctrine is established through the united voice of the First Presidency and Quorum of the Twelve, not through one person's opinion.

3.definition: Church governance distinguishes between binding, sustained counsel from the First Presidency and Quorum of the Twelve and the individual opinions or applications of local leaders. Faithful members learn to respect both while understanding the difference in authority.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A bishop implements a ward-level rule that goes beyond church handbook guidelines, and members are divided about whether to comply.
3.scenarioYouDo: You notice that what's emphasized in your ward sometimes reflects your bishop's priorities more than official church direction, and you want to navigate that wisely.

challenge.scenario: Your bishop introduces a standard that isn't in the General Handbook and feels overly restrictive. What's the best approach?
options: Obey without question because a bishop's word is law
Rebel openly and refuse to attend
Respectfully seek clarification while recognizing that official church policy — established by the First Presidency — takes precedence over local additions
Assume the bishop has received special revelation that trumps the handbook

3.tip: The General Handbook exists for a reason — it protects members from both over-reach and under-engagement.

1.definition: Reading scriptures isn't just scanning the words — it means really thinking about them and letting them fill you up, like eating a delicious meal.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your friend reads a whole chapter in two minutes just to check it off their list, but can't remember a single thing from it.
1.scenarioYouDo: You read two verses slowly and think about what they mean, and you feel something in your heart — even though it wasn't very many verses.

challenge.scenario: What's the best way to study scriptures?
options: Read as fast as possible to finish quickly
Read a little bit and really think about what it means
Only read when you're in trouble

1.tip: Nephi said to 'feast upon the words of Christ' — not snack on them (2 Nephi 32:3).

2.definition: Feasting on the word means actively engaging with scripture — pondering, asking questions, looking for patterns, and applying what you read to your own life.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A friend reads her scriptures every day but treats it like homework — checking off a box without ever stopping to think about what she's reading.
2.scenarioYouDo: You realize you've been reading scriptures on autopilot and wonder how to make it matter more.

challenge.scenario: Your scripture study feels dry and routine. What's the best way to make it meaningful again?
options: Read more chapters faster
Start reading with a question in mind and look for answers as you study
Take a break from scriptures until you feel spiritual again
Switch to only listening to podcasts about the scriptures instead

2.tip: Bringing a question to your reading changes everything — it turns passive reading into active revelation.

3.definition: Feasting on the scriptures means engaging deeply — cross-referencing, using footnotes, pondering context, looking for types and shadows, and seeking personal messages from God through the text.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A returned missionary admits that after their mission, scripture study became a chore and they lost the deep engagement they once had.
3.scenarioYouDo: You've read the Book of Mormon several times but feel like you're not getting new insights anymore.

challenge.scenario: You've read the Book of Mormon multiple times and feel you've 'heard it all.' How do you go deeper?
options: Move on to other books since you've mastered this one
Try new study methods — topical study, cross-referencing, studying in a different language, or reading from a specific character's perspective
Just read it faster each time
Accept that scripture study loses its value over time

3.tip: The scriptures don't change, but you do — and each time you return, you bring new eyes and new questions.

1.definition: When you read your scriptures with a question in your heart, it's like asking Heavenly Father to help you find an answer — and He often does!
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your friend was worried about a problem at school, read her scriptures with that question in mind, and found a verse that helped her know what to do.
1.scenarioYouDo: You're starting your scripture reading and you think, 'What does Heavenly Father want me to learn today?' Then a verse really stands out to you.

challenge.scenario: You open your scriptures but don't know where to start. What's a good idea?
options: Just close the book and try again later
Think of a question you have and look for answers while you read
Open to a random page and hope for the best

1.tip: A question turns your reading into a conversation with Heavenly Father.

2.definition: Question-driven study transforms scripture reading from a routine into a personal quest. When you bring honest questions, the Spirit can direct you to answers.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A seminary teacher challenges the class to write down one question before each reading session, and students start finding answers they never noticed before.
2.scenarioYouDo: You start writing a question at the top of your study journal each day, and you notice you get way more out of your reading.

challenge.scenario: You want to improve your scripture study. Which method would be most effective?
options: Set a timer and read as fast as you can
Skim through and highlight random verses that look cool
Begin each session with a specific question and actively search for insights as you read
Only read the chapter headings to save time

2.tip: Elder Bednar taught that asking inspired questions is one of the most powerful study skills you can develop.

3.definition: Approaching scripture with intentional questions is a form of inquiry-based revelation. It mirrors the pattern Joseph Smith used — he went to the scriptures with questions and received answers that changed the world.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A friend in your institute class shares that she plans her scripture study around questions from her week — decisions, concerns, or doctrinal puzzles — and it transforms her reading.
3.scenarioYouDo: You've been studying the same chapters for a class assignment, but when you bring a personal struggle to the text, entirely new verses come alive.

challenge.scenario: You need to study a block of scripture for a class, but you've read it before and it feels stale. What's the most effective approach?
options: Just power through it to fulfill the assignment
Accept that repeated readings have diminishing returns
Bring a specific personal question or lens to the text — the same chapter yields different insights when you approach it with different questions
Ask a friend to summarize it for you instead

3.tip: Joseph Smith's first question — 'Which church should I join?' — was born from James 1:5. Your questions can be just as powerful.

1.definition: The Book of Mormon is like the keystone in an arch — it's the piece that holds everything together. It helps us know that Jesus is real and that the gospel is true.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your teacher shows a picture of a stone arch and takes out the top stone, and the whole thing falls apart — then explains that's what the Book of Mormon is for the church.
1.scenarioYouDo: You read a story in the Book of Mormon about Jesus visiting people and it makes your heart feel warm and happy.

challenge.scenario: Why is the Book of Mormon called the 'keystone' of our religion?
options: Because it's the oldest book
Because it holds everything else together — it testifies of Jesus and confirms the truth of the gospel
Because it was written by Joseph Smith from his own ideas

1.tip: Joseph Smith called the Book of Mormon 'the keystone of our religion' — pull it out and everything else weakens.

2.definition: The Book of Mormon is the keystone — it testifies of Christ, validates Joseph Smith's prophetic calling, and contains the fullness of the gospel. Everything connects back to it.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A friend asks why Latter-day Saints care so much about the Book of Mormon when they already have the Bible.
2.scenarioYouDo: You're explaining your beliefs to someone and realize you need to explain why the Book of Mormon matters so much.

challenge.scenario: A classmate asks, 'Why do you need the Book of Mormon when you already have the Bible?' What's the best answer?
options: We don't really need the Bible — just the Book of Mormon
The Book of Mormon is another testament of Jesus Christ that supports and clarifies biblical teachings through a second witness
It's just another old book with interesting stories
The Book of Mormon replaces the Bible entirely

2.tip: Two witnesses are stronger than one (2 Corinthians 13:1). The Book of Mormon is Christ's second witness.

3.definition: The Book of Mormon's role as keystone means it's the fulcrum of Latter-day Saint truth claims: if it's true, Joseph Smith was a prophet, the Restoration happened, and the gospel is on the earth. Moroni 10:3-5 invites everyone to test this claim personally.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A peer in your religion class argues that the Book of Mormon can't be verified archaeologically, so it shouldn't be trusted.
3.scenarioYouDo: You're challenged to defend the Book of Mormon's value, and you realize the most powerful evidence isn't external — it's the personal witness the Spirit provides.

challenge.scenario: Someone says the Book of Mormon can't be proven by science, so it can't be true. What's the most thoughtful response?
options: Argue that there is definitive archaeological proof
Dismiss science entirely as irrelevant to faith
Acknowledge the limits of current evidence while inviting them to test Moroni's promise — spiritual knowledge and empirical knowledge operate in complementary ways
Agree that it must be fiction since there's no physical proof

3.tip: Moroni 10:3-5 is an invitation to experiment, not a demand for blind acceptance.

1.definition: The footnotes and cross-references in your scriptures are like secret passages — they connect verses across different books and help you see the bigger picture.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your older sibling shows you a footnote in the Book of Mormon that connects to a verse in the Bible, and you realize they're telling the same story!
1.scenarioYouDo: You click a footnote in the scripture app and discover a verse you never knew existed — and it explains exactly what you were confused about.

challenge.scenario: You don't understand a verse. What's a good thing to try?
options: Skip it and move on
Look at the footnotes to see if other verses help explain it
Decide the scriptures are too confusing

1.tip: Footnotes are like clues in a treasure hunt — they lead you to even more cool discoveries.

2.definition: Cross-referencing means connecting related verses across different books of scripture. It deepens understanding and shows how scripture is a unified whole.
2.scenarioDoneTo: In seminary, a student links a verse from Isaiah to one in the Book of Mormon and one in the Doctrine and Covenants, showing how the same truth is repeated across thousands of years.
2.scenarioYouDo: You start using the scripture app's cross-reference tool and realize that the Bible, Book of Mormon, and Doctrine and Covenants are all talking to each other.

challenge.scenario: You want to understand a principle more deeply. Which study skill would help most?
options: Only read one verse about it and stop
Look up cross-references to see how the same principle is taught in different books of scripture
Ask a friend to explain it instead of studying yourself
Read a commentary online and skip the actual scriptures

2.tip: When you see the same truth in Genesis, Alma, and D&C, you know it matters to God.

3.definition: Skilled cross-referencing reveals typology, prophecy fulfillment, and doctrinal threads across the standard works. It transforms surface-level reading into theological literacy.
3.scenarioDoneTo: An institute instructor walks through how the Abrahamic covenant is echoed in 2 Nephi, reiterated in D&C 132, and fulfilled in modern temple ordinances — and the class sees scripture as a unified document for the first time.
3.scenarioYouDo: You trace a single theme — like covenant — across all four standard works and realize how coherently they connect across centuries and authors.

challenge.scenario: You want to build a deep understanding of the Atonement. What's the most effective study approach?
options: Read one talk from General Conference and call it good
Read only Isaiah because it's the most challenging
Trace the Atonement across multiple books of scripture — Isaiah 53, Mosiah 3, Alma 7, 2 Nephi 9, D&C 19 — and synthesize the picture each adds
Stick to the New Testament only since that's where the Atonement happened

3.tip: The standard works are four witnesses telling one story. Cross-referencing lets you hear the harmony.

1.definition: Scriptures aren't just old stories — they are meant to help you right now, with your own problems and choices.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Nephi had to be brave when his brothers were mean. Your teacher says, 'You might have to be brave too — like at recess when someone isn't being nice.'
1.scenarioYouDo: You read about Nephi going to get the brass plates, and you realize you need courage to do something hard at school too.

challenge.scenario: You read a story about a Book of Mormon hero being brave. What should you do with that?
options: Just think, 'Cool story' and close the book
Think about how you can be brave like that in your own life
Decide those stories only matter for people who lived long ago

1.tip: Nephi said to 'liken all scriptures unto us' — that means they're really about you (1 Nephi 19:23).

2.definition: The point of scripture study isn't just knowledge — it's transformation. Likening scriptures to yourself means seeing how the principles apply to your real, current circumstances.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A friend reads about the people of Ammon burying their weapons and realizes it applies to her own struggle with giving up a bad habit.
2.scenarioYouDo: You read about Alma the Younger's conversion and start thinking about your own need for change and repentance.

challenge.scenario: You finish reading a chapter and want to apply it, but it's about ancient war. How can it be relevant?
options: It's not relevant — skip it
Look for the underlying principle — like courage, trust in God, or preparation — and apply that to your life
Only apply scriptures that directly mention modern situations
Wait for your seminary teacher to tell you what it means

2.tip: Every scripture has a surface story and a deeper principle. The principle is always relevant.

3.definition: Personal application is where scripture becomes transformative. Looking for 'types' — patterns, symbols, and parallels — trains you to see your own life story within the scriptural narrative.
3.scenarioDoneTo: An institute student shares that the wilderness journey in 1 Nephi became deeply personal when she saw it as a metaphor for her own faith transition after a family crisis.
3.scenarioYouDo: You begin seeing your own challenges reflected in the scriptural patterns — exodus, wilderness, promised land — and it gives your struggles meaning.

challenge.scenario: You're going through a personal 'wilderness' season of struggle and uncertainty. How can scripture help?
options: Scriptures can't help with real, modern problems
Look only for cheerful, uplifting verses to feel better
Identify scriptural patterns that mirror your experience — Israel's wilderness, Lehi's journey, Alma's prison — and draw strength from how God worked in those narratives
Read scriptures only about success to remind yourself how things should be

3.tip: If you're in the wilderness, you're in good company — most of God's greatest people spent time there too.

1.definition: If you build good spiritual habits now — like praying and reading scriptures — you'll be ready when hard times come.
1.scenarioDoneTo: The wise man in Jesus's story built his house on rock before the storm came. When the rain hit, his house stayed strong.
1.scenarioYouDo: You've been praying every night, and when something scary happens, you already know how to talk to Heavenly Father about it.

challenge.scenario: When is the best time to start building strong spiritual habits?
options: Only when something bad happens
Right now — before you need them
When you're older and life gets harder

1.tip: Jesus taught about building on a rock (Matthew 7:24-27) — the time to build is before the rain comes.

2.definition: Spiritual resilience means preparing your foundation before challenges hit. The habits you build now are what will hold you when life gets hard.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A friend who never really studied the scriptures or developed a prayer life suddenly faces a family crisis and feels totally unequipped spiritually.
2.scenarioYouDo: Things are going well right now, and you're tempted to coast on spiritual things — but you know a storm could come anytime.

challenge.scenario: Your life feels pretty good right now. Why should you invest in spiritual habits?
options: You shouldn't — wait until you actually need them
Because spiritual foundations are built during calm times, not crisis times
Spiritual habits are only for people going through hard things
You only need the basics — deep study is overkill

2.tip: You don't build a shelter during a hurricane. Prepare now.

3.definition: Spiritual resilience is proactive — it's cultivated during seasons of peace so it's available during seasons of trial. Waiting to develop a spiritual foundation until crisis hits is like learning to swim in a flood.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A returned missionary with years of deep study and prayer faces a major faith crisis but finds their reservoir of spiritual experiences gives them something to hold onto.
3.scenarioYouDo: You see friends struggling after encountering challenging information, and you realize your consistent study and prayer have quietly given you a foundation they didn't build.

challenge.scenario: You're in a stable, peaceful season of life. How should you approach spiritual preparation?
options: Cruise — you can build your foundation when you need it
Invest deeply now — build a reservoir of experience, knowledge, and spiritual practice that you can draw on when trials come
Focus only on intellectual study; spiritual habits aren't as important
Assume that past spiritual experiences will carry you forever without maintenance

3.tip: President Nelson: 'The time to prepare for a trial is before it comes. We build our spiritual reserves in advance.'

1.definition: Sometimes, even when you're trying hard, you might feel far from Heavenly Father. That's normal — it doesn't mean you did anything wrong.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your older brother says he prayed but didn't feel anything, and he wonders if God is listening. Mom tells him that even Jesus felt alone once.
1.scenarioYouDo: You say your prayers but feel nothing, and you wonder if Heavenly Father forgot about you.

challenge.scenario: You pray and pray but feel nothing. What should you remember?
options: God must not love you anymore
Feeling distant from God sometimes is normal — keep praying, He's still there
You should stop praying since it's not working

1.tip: Even Jesus cried out, 'Why hast thou forsaken me?' Feeling alone doesn't mean you are alone.

2.definition: A 'dark night of the soul' is a period when you feel spiritually empty or disconnected despite your best efforts. It's a normal part of faith — not a sign of failure.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A seminary classmate breaks down and admits she hasn't felt the Spirit in months and thinks God has given up on her.
2.scenarioYouDo: You've been doing everything 'right' — scripture study, prayer, church — but you feel absolutely nothing spiritually, and you're scared.

challenge.scenario: You've been faithful but feel spiritually empty for weeks. What's the truth?
options: You must have committed some hidden sin
Spiritual dry seasons are a normal part of the faith journey — they test and deepen your commitment
God is punishing you for something
You've lost the Spirit permanently

2.tip: Some of the greatest spiritual growth happens in the silence. Hold on.

3.definition: The 'dark night' is a well-documented spiritual phenomenon where God allows a season of silence to deepen faith beyond feelings. It's not punishment — it's often an invitation to develop faith that doesn't depend on constant spiritual confirmation.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A mission companion confided that she went months without feeling the Spirit, despite teaching and serving every day, and nearly went home — but pushing through became the most transformative experience of her life.
3.scenarioYouDo: You're in a prolonged season of spiritual silence. You're doing everything you know to do, but heaven feels closed, and you're questioning everything.

challenge.scenario: You're in a months-long spiritual dry spell despite consistent faithfulness. How do you interpret this?
options: It proves God isn't real
It's punishment for secret unworthiness
Recognize it as a normal, even purposeful, phase of spiritual development — God may be inviting you to walk by faith rather than feeling, deepening a trust that doesn't depend on constant reassurance
Assume you'll never feel the Spirit again and adjust your expectations permanently

3.tip: Elder Holland: 'Don't you quit. You keep walking. You keep trying. There is help and happiness ahead.'

1.definition: If someone you love stops coming to church, you can still love them just as much — and you can keep going to church yourself.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your older cousin stops coming to church and some kids say mean things about her. Your parents say, 'We love her no matter what.'
1.scenarioYouDo: Your best friend's family leaves the church, and you wonder if you should stop going too so they won't feel bad.

challenge.scenario: Your friend leaves the church. What should you do?
options: Stop being their friend
Keep loving them and keep going to church yourself
Leave the church too so they don't feel alone

1.tip: Loving someone and agreeing with all their choices are two different things — and you can always do the first one.

2.definition: When people you love leave the church, it can shake your own faith. But their choice doesn't have to determine yours — love them unconditionally AND maintain your own spiritual path.
2.scenarioDoneTo: An older sibling leaves the church and family dinners become tense. You feel caught between loyalty to your sibling and your own beliefs.
2.scenarioYouDo: A close friend tells you the church isn't true and expects you to agree. You feel pressure to follow them or risk losing the friendship.

challenge.scenario: Your older sibling leaves the church and seems happier outside it. How do you respond?
options: Decide the church must be wrong if they left
Love them completely, respect their journey, and continue to honestly follow your own spiritual convictions
Cut them off until they come back
Pretend you agree with them to keep the peace

2.tip: Someone else's path doesn't invalidate yours. You can love people and walk a different road.

3.definition: Navigating the departure of loved ones from the church requires emotional maturity — holding space for their pain and choices while not surrendering your own spiritual foundation. Love without conditions; follow your own conscience without apology.
3.scenarioDoneTo: Your spouse or very close family member announces they no longer believe, and your entire social and spiritual world shifts overnight.
3.scenarioYouDo: Several friends you respected deeply have left the church, and their reasons are compelling. You're questioning whether staying is the right choice.

challenge.scenario: Multiple people you respect have left the church and shared persuasive reasons. How do you navigate this?
options: Leave too, since smart people you admire have left
Stay only out of fear, tradition, or social pressure
Honestly examine their concerns, but ultimately base your decision on your own spiritual experiences, study, and relationship with God — not on others' choices
Avoid learning about their reasons so your testimony isn't threatened

3.tip: You can listen to everyone's story and still write your own. Your testimony belongs to you.

1.definition: When something confuses you about the gospel, hold on to what you DO know — like that Heavenly Father loves you and answers prayers.
1.scenarioDoneTo: A kid at school asks you a question about your church you can't answer, and you feel worried. But then you remember: 'I know Heavenly Father loves me.'
1.scenarioYouDo: You hear something confusing about the church and feel shaky, but then you remember the time you felt the Spirit, and it helps you feel safe.

challenge.scenario: You hear something confusing about the church. What should you do?
options: Forget everything you've ever felt
Hold on to what you already know is true and keep learning
Decide the whole thing must be wrong

1.tip: When it's dark, you hold on to the last light you saw — don't let go of what you know.

2.definition: When new information challenges your faith, don't throw away everything in a panic. Hold to what you know — your spiritual experiences are real — while you work through the questions.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A classmate shares a troubling church history fact, and you feel your whole testimony wobbling. But then you remember the powerful spiritual experience you had at camp.
2.scenarioYouDo: You encounter something about church history that bothers you, and you're tempted to toss everything — but you pause and remember what you've already felt.

challenge.scenario: You learn something that shakes your faith. What's the healthiest first step?
options: Immediately abandon all your beliefs
Anchor to the spiritual experiences you've already had while honestly seeking answers to the new question
Refuse to think about it and suppress the concern
Decide that feelings aren't evidence of anything

2.tip: Elder Uchtdorf: 'Doubt your doubts before you doubt your faith.'

3.definition: Spiritual resilience involves refusing to abandon confirmed truths in the face of unresolved questions. Your spiritual experiences constitute real data — they deserve the same respect you give to new, troubling information.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A graduate student reads a critical book about the church and feels their entire testimony crumbling — until they realize they're weighing new criticism more heavily than years of personal spiritual experience.
3.scenarioYouDo: You encounter a genuinely difficult historical or doctrinal issue, and you're tempted to let this one issue invalidate every spiritual witness you've ever received.

challenge.scenario: You discover a church history issue you can't immediately resolve. How should you weight it against your existing spiritual experiences?
options: One unanswered question outweighs every previous spiritual experience
Suppress the question and pretend it doesn't exist
Give honest weight to both — your spiritual experiences are real data, and an unresolved question doesn't erase them. Hold what you know while you work through what you don't
Only accept information that confirms what you already believe

3.tip: A thousand answered prayers don't become worthless because of one unanswered question.

1.definition: When something really hard happens — like a family problem or losing a friend — your faith can help you stay strong, like a tree with deep roots in a big wind.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your friend's parents got divorced, and she was really sad. But she said praying helped her feel less alone during the hard parts.
1.scenarioYouDo: Something really scary or sad happens and you feel like everything is falling apart — but then you pray and feel a little bit of peace.

challenge.scenario: Something really hard happens in your life. What should you do?
options: Decide Heavenly Father doesn't care about you
Turn to Him in prayer — He's especially close during hard times
Handle it all by yourself and don't tell anyone

1.tip: God doesn't always stop the storm, but He'll hold you through it.

2.definition: During a personal crisis, your faith can either be your anchor or something you abandon. Staying grounded means leaning into your spiritual practices instead of away from them.
2.scenarioDoneTo: After a family tragedy, a friend stopped going to church because she couldn't understand why God would let it happen — and later admitted that leaving didn't help either.
2.scenarioYouDo: Something devastating happens and your first instinct is to blame God and pull away from everything spiritual.

challenge.scenario: You experience a painful loss and feel angry at God. What's the healthiest response?
options: Walk away from faith entirely
Be honest about your anger while staying connected — bring your pain to God rather than walking away from Him
Pretend you're fine and don't feel anything
Decide God doesn't exist because bad things happened

2.tip: God can handle your anger. He'd rather you bring it to Him than take it away.

3.definition: Faith crises and life crises often converge. Spiritual resilience means having the maturity to lean into faith during suffering rather than abandoning it — while also being honest about the hard questions suffering raises.
3.scenarioDoneTo: Someone you respect loses a child and, instead of losing faith, becomes more compassionate and spiritually deep — not because the experience was 'good,' but because they brought their grief to God instead of away from Him.
3.scenarioYouDo: You face a situation that makes God's plan feel cruel or meaningless, and you must decide whether to engage your faith or discard it.

challenge.scenario: A devastating event makes you question whether God's plan is real or good. How do you stay grounded?
options: Conclude that suffering disproves God's existence
Refuse to acknowledge the pain and insist everything is fine
Accept that suffering raises legitimate theological questions while choosing to bring those questions into your faith rather than using them to abandon it — honesty and faith can coexist
Use religion to bypass the grief entirely and skip to 'It's all part of the plan'

3.tip: Faith isn't the absence of hard questions — it's the decision to keep walking with God while you carry them.

1.definition: Heavenly Father gave you the power to make your own choices. That's one of His greatest gifts — and it's why you're here on Earth.
1.scenarioDoneTo: In the pre-earth life, Satan wanted to force everyone to be good, but Heavenly Father chose Jesus's plan, which lets us choose for ourselves.
1.scenarioYouDo: Every day, you get to choose — to be kind or mean, to try or give up, to follow Jesus or not. That choice is yours.

challenge.scenario: Why did Heavenly Father give us the power to choose?
options: So we could learn and grow by making our own decisions
Because He doesn't care what we do
He didn't — Satan's plan was to give us choices

1.tip: Agency is God's greatest gift — it means He trusts you enough to let you choose.

2.definition: Agency — the ability to choose — is the foundational gift of God's plan. Without it, growth, love, and true faithfulness would be impossible.
2.scenarioDoneTo: In a class discussion, someone argues that it would be better if God just made everyone be good. Another student explains why that would destroy the whole point of being here.
2.scenarioYouDo: You realize that every meaningful choice — faith, kindness, repentance — only matters because you had the freedom to choose otherwise.

challenge.scenario: Someone says, 'If God really loved us, He would stop us from making bad choices.' What's the flaw in that logic?
options: There's no flaw — God should stop bad choices
Removing our ability to choose would destroy our ability to grow, love, and become like God — agency is essential to the plan
Bad choices don't exist — everything is relative
God can't actually stop us even if He wanted to

2.tip: Forced obedience isn't righteousness. God wants willing hearts, not programmed robots (2 Nephi 2:27).

3.definition: Agency is the metaphysical foundation of God's plan. Lehi taught that without opposition and the freedom to choose, 'there is no existence' of righteousness, joy, or meaning (2 Nephi 2:11-16). Agency makes real love — and real faith — possible.
3.scenarioDoneTo: In an ethics class, the professor argues that a deterministic universe undermines moral responsibility. You realize Latter-day Saint theology addresses this directly through the doctrine of agency.
3.scenarioYouDo: You're thinking deeply about why God allows suffering, and you realize the same agency that allows evil is what makes genuine love, growth, and choosing God meaningful.

challenge.scenario: A friend argues that an all-powerful God who allows suffering is either not powerful or not good. How does the doctrine of agency address this?
options: It doesn't — the problem of evil has no answer
Agency means God chose to allow meaningful moral freedom even though it permits suffering — because without it, love, growth, and righteousness would be impossible
God allows suffering to punish people
Suffering is just an illusion and doesn't need explaining

3.tip: Lehi's great insight: opposition isn't a flaw in the plan — it IS the plan (2 Nephi 2:11).

1.definition: When you make a choice, the results come back to you — good or bad. That's what accountability means.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your brother blames his friend for breaking a rule, but Mom says, 'You chose to follow along — that's on you.'
1.scenarioYouDo: You do something wrong and try to blame your sister, but you know deep down it was your choice.

challenge.scenario: You broke a family rule but your sibling dared you to do it. Whose fault is it?
options: Your sibling's fault — they dared you
Yours — you chose to do it even though someone else suggested it
Nobody's fault — dares don't count

1.tip: You always get to choose, and your choices always have consequences — that's how Heavenly Father's plan works.

2.definition: Agency and accountability are two sides of the same coin. The freedom to choose comes with the responsibility for those choices — you can't have one without the other.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A friend cheats on a test and blames the teacher for making it too hard. Another friend says, 'You still chose to cheat.'
2.scenarioYouDo: You make a poor decision under peer pressure and feel tempted to blame the situation — but you know the choice was ultimately yours.

challenge.scenario: You give in to peer pressure and break a personal standard. How do you take accountability?
options: Blame the people who pressured you
Own your choice, acknowledge the consequence, and commit to doing better — accountability is what makes growth possible
Pretend it never happened
Decide personal standards are unrealistic and lower them

2.tip: Accountability isn't punishment — it's the foundation of personal growth and repentance.

3.definition: Accountability means accepting the full moral weight of your choices. Mormon theology holds that light and knowledge increase accountability — the more you know, the more responsible you are for acting on it.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A friend rationalizes dishonest behavior by blaming circumstances: 'Everyone does it,' 'The system is unfair,' 'I had no choice.' But on reflection, they realize they chose convenience over integrity.
3.scenarioYouDo: You're in a situation where doing the wrong thing is easy and doing the right thing is costly, and you're tempted to find someone or something else to blame.

challenge.scenario: You face a situation where doing the ethical thing is costly and no one would know if you cut corners. How does accountability apply?
options: If no one knows, it doesn't matter
Accountability is between you and God — your choices shape your character regardless of who's watching (Alma 12:14)
Only worry about accountability when there are consequences from others
Rules are made by humans so they're not spiritually binding

3.tip: 'For our words will condemn us… and our thoughts will also condemn us' (Alma 12:14). Accountability is internal.

1.definition: You can't control what other people do, but you can always choose how you react. That's your superpower.
1.scenarioDoneTo: A kid at school says something mean to your friend. Your friend takes a deep breath and walks away instead of saying something mean back.
1.scenarioYouDo: Someone teases you and you want to yell, but you choose to stay calm and talk to a teacher instead.

challenge.scenario: Someone at school is being mean to you. What should you do?
options: Be mean right back — they deserve it
Choose to stay calm and respond with kindness or walk away
Let them keep being mean and don't tell anyone

1.tip: Your reaction is the one thing nobody can take from you — choose it wisely.

2.definition: True agency includes the ability to choose your response to anything that happens to you. Others control their actions — you control your reaction.
2.scenarioDoneTo: Two friends get the same unfair grade. One spirals into bitterness, and the other calmly talks to the teacher. Same circumstances — different choices.
2.scenarioYouDo: A friend betrays your trust and you're furious. You want revenge, but you realize the only thing you can actually control is how you respond.

challenge.scenario: A friend spreads a rumor about you and you feel humiliated and angry. What's the best use of your agency?
options: Spread a worse rumor about them
React publicly and make a huge scene
Choose your response carefully — address it directly with the person if needed, but don't let their choices dictate your character
Pretend it didn't happen and bury your feelings

2.tip: Between what happens to you and how you respond, there is a space — and in that space is your agency.

3.definition: Viktor Frankl wrote, 'Between stimulus and response there is a space. In that space is our freedom and our power to choose.' This principle is deeply scriptural — agency means we are never merely victims of our circumstances.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A coworker is promoted unfairly over you. Two versions of you exist: one who lets resentment shape their entire career, and one who chooses to excel regardless of the injustice.
3.scenarioYouDo: Someone close to you causes serious harm, and you're faced with the choice: let bitterness define you, or exercise the agency to choose healing, boundaries, and growth.

challenge.scenario: Someone has genuinely wronged you, and the situation is profoundly unfair. How do you exercise agency?
options: You're a victim of circumstance — agency doesn't apply when someone else caused the harm
Forgive immediately and pretend nothing happened, bypassing real processing
Acknowledge the hurt honestly, then choose your path forward — set boundaries, seek healing, and refuse to let someone else's actions define your identity or your relationship with God
Seek revenge to restore balance

3.tip: You can't rewrite what happened to you, but you are always the author of what happens next.

1.definition: To make good choices, you need to learn and understand what your options are. That's why Heavenly Father wants you to learn as much as you can.
1.scenarioDoneTo: Your mom lets you choose a snack, but first she explains which ones are healthy and which ones are mostly sugar, so you can pick wisely.
1.scenarioYouDo: You learn about the Word of Wisdom in Primary, and now when someone offers you something, you actually know why you say no.

challenge.scenario: Why does Heavenly Father want you to learn new things?
options: He doesn't care if you learn or not
Because the more you know, the better choices you can make
Because learning is just homework and chores

1.tip: Knowledge is like a flashlight — it helps you see what's ahead so you can choose the right path.

2.definition: True agency requires knowledge. You can't really choose if you don't understand your options. That's why learning, studying, and seeking truth are essential — they empower your ability to choose.
2.scenarioDoneTo: A friend makes a major life decision without doing any research, and it goes badly. She realizes she chose blindly instead of wisely.
2.scenarioYouDo: You face a big decision and realize that studying your options — including what the scriptures and the Spirit say — is what makes your choice truly free.

challenge.scenario: You face an important decision. What makes your choice a true exercise of agency?
options: Go with gut instinct only
Making the decision based on what your friends are doing
Seeking knowledge, counsel, and spiritual guidance so your choice is informed and intentional
Flipping a coin because the outcome doesn't matter

2.tip: 'The glory of God is intelligence' (D&C 93:36). Knowledge doesn't limit your choices — it enlarges them.

3.definition: Agency without knowledge is merely reaction. The scriptures emphasize that truth sets us free (John 8:32) — genuine moral agency requires understanding consequences, alternatives, and eternal principles. Seeking truth is a sacred responsibility.
3.scenarioDoneTo: A peer makes a consequential faith decision based solely on a viral social media post, without studying primary sources, praying, or consulting trusted mentors.
3.scenarioYouDo: You realize that some of your most important beliefs have been inherited rather than examined, and you feel called to do the honest work of understanding them for yourself.

challenge.scenario: You hold beliefs you've never deeply examined. What does responsible agency look like?
options: Examined faith is dangerous — just believe what you were taught
Reject everything you inherited and start from scratch
Do the honest work of studying, praying, and seeking truth — so your faith is truly your own and your choices are informed by understanding, not just habit
Outsource your beliefs to whoever is most persuasive

3.tip: An unexamined faith is fragile. A tested faith is unshakeable. 'Study it out in your mind' (D&C 9:8).
